Use S3 client-side encryption with keys generated is incorrect because client-side encryption requires the application to manage the encryption process and Secrets Manager adds complexity while losing the S3-native encryption integration.

question: A government agency requires that all data stored in S3 remains within a specific AWS Region and cannot be replicated or accessed from outside that Region. The agency also needs to ensure that no S3 API calls originate from outside the agency VPC. Which combination of controls enforces these requirements?
options: A. Create an S3 bucket policy with a condition restricting access to the agency VPC endpoint using aws:sourceVpce and enable S3 Block Public Access at the account level to prevent public access
B. Create an S3 bucket in the required Region and rely on the default behavior that S3 data stays in the Region where the bucket was created without additional controls since S3 never moves data
C. Create an S3 bucket policy with a condition restricting access to requests from the agency VPC endpoint and add an SCP that denies s3:PutBucketReplication to prevent any cross-Region replication
D. Configure S3 Object Lock in compliance mode on all objects in the bucket which prevents data from being moved or replicated outside the original Region for the duration of the lock period
explanation: Create an S3 bucket policy is correct because the VPC endpoint condition in the bucket policy ensures all API calls come through the agency VPC and the SCP preventing PutBucketReplication ensures data cannot be replicated outside the Region. Create an S3 bucket policy is incorrect because while it restricts access to the VPC endpoint it does not prevent an administrator from configuring cross-Region replication which would copy data outside the Region.

question: A machine learning company uploads large training datasets to S3 from on-premises data centers over a 1 Gbps Direct Connect link. Uploading a 500 GB dataset takes over 2 hours. The company wants to reduce upload time. Which S3 upload optimization reduces the transfer time most effectively?
options: A. Use S3 Transfer Acceleration which routes uploads through CloudFront edge locations using optimized network paths to reduce latency and increase throughput for long-distance transfers
B. Request a 10 Gbps Direct Connect connection to increase the available bandwidth tenfold which directly reduces the upload time proportionally without any application-level changes
C. Compress the dataset using gzip before uploading to S3 to reduce the total data transferred over the Direct Connect link then decompress using a Lambda function after upload completes
D. Use S3 multipart upload to split the 500 GB dataset into parts and upload them in parallel across multiple threads to maximize utilization of the 1 Gbps Direct Connect bandwidth
explanation: Request a 10 Gbps Direct Connect connection to increase is correct because multipart upload with parallel threads maximizes the utilization of the existing 1 Gbps bandwidth by uploading multiple parts simultaneously which is the most effective optimization without infrastructure changes. Use S3 Transfer Acceleration is incorrect because transfer Acceleration is most beneficial for internet-based transfers and may not improve speeds over Direct Connect which already uses dedicated network paths. Compress the dataset is incorrect because machine learning training data is often already in binary formats that do not compress significantly and the compression and decompression add processing time.

question: An e-commerce company hosts static website assets in S3 including product images CSS and JavaScript files. The website is served globally through CloudFront. The company wants to deploy updated assets without causing broken pages for users who have cached references to old asset filenames. Which deployment strategy prevents broken references during updates?
options: A. Use S3 Versioning and update CloudFront to reference specific version IDs in the origin path so that old and new asset versions coexist and users can access both during the transition period
B. Upload new assets with unique filenames that include a content hash and update the HTML references to point to the new filenames so old and new assets coexist in S3 simultaneously
C. Create a CloudFront invalidation for all changed files immediately after uploading new assets to S3 to ensure edge caches serve the updated versions within seconds of deployment
D. Configure S3 object expiration headers to set a very short cache TTL of 10 seconds on all assets so CloudFront frequently checks S3 for updated versions and serves fresh content
explanation: Upload new assets is correct because content-hashed filenames ensure old and new assets coexist in S3 and users with cached HTML pages referencing old filenames continue to work while new page loads reference new filenames. Use S3 Versioning is incorrect because cloudFront origin paths do not support S3 version IDs and S3 Versioning does not solve the filename reference problem in cached HTML. Create a CloudFront invalidation for all changed files is incorrect because cloudFront invalidation takes time to propagate globally and during that window users may get a mix of old HTML and new assets or vice versa.

question: A backup solutions company needs to store customer backup data in S3 with the lowest possible cost. Backups are written once and only retrieved in disaster recovery scenarios which occur less than once per year. Retrieval time of up to 12 hours is acceptable during recovery. Which S3 storage class minimizes cost for this use case?
options: A. S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval which provides low-cost storage with retrieval options ranging from minutes to hours and supports bulk retrieval for large datasets at reduced cost
B. S3 Glacier Deep Archive which provides the lowest cost storage in AWS with retrieval available within 12 hours suitable for data that is rarely accessed and can tolerate long retrieval times
C. S3 Standard-Infrequent Access which provides lower storage costs than S3 Standard with millisecond retrieval time and is designed for data accessed less frequently than once per month
D. S3 Intelligent-Tiering which automatically optimizes storage costs by moving data between frequent and infrequent access tiers based on changing access patterns without retrieval charges
explanation: S3 Glacier Deep Archive is correct because S3 Glacier Deep Archive offers the lowest storage cost in AWS and its standard retrieval time of up to 12 hours meets the acceptable recovery time for disaster scenarios that occur less than once per year. S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval is incorrect because glacier Flexible Retrieval costs more than Deep Archive and the faster retrieval options are not needed when 12 hours is acceptable. S3 Standard-Infrequent Access is incorrect because standard-IA has significantly higher storage costs than Glacier Deep Archive and millisecond retrieval is unnecessary for disaster recovery backups.

question: An order processing system uses an SQS Standard queue to decouple order submission from fulfillment. During peak sales events orders occasionally process twice because SQS Standard delivers messages at least once. Duplicate fulfillment causes customer complaints and financial losses. The company needs exactly-once processing without changing the queue type. Which approach prevents duplicate order processing?
options: A. Reduce the SQS visibility timeout to 5 seconds so that messages are reprocessed quickly if the first processing attempt fails reducing the window where duplicates can occur in the system
B. Configure a dead-letter queue with a maximum receive count of 1 so that after the first processing attempt the message moves to the DLQ preventing any possibility of reprocessing the same order
C. Enable long polling on the SQS queue with a wait time of 20 seconds which reduces empty responses and prevents the consumer from receiving the same message multiple times in rapid succession
D. Implement idempotency in the order processing application by storing processed order IDs in a DynamoDB table and checking for duplicates before processing each message from the queue
explanation: Configure a dead-letter queue is correct because implementing idempotency with a DynamoDB lookup ensures that even if SQS delivers a message multiple times the application processes each order exactly once by detecting and skipping duplicates. Reduce the SQS visibility timeout to 5 seconds so is incorrect because reducing the visibility timeout increases the likelihood of duplicate processing since messages become visible again sooner if processing takes longer than 5 seconds. Enable long polling on the SQS queue is incorrect because long polling optimizes empty receive calls but does not prevent the at-least-once delivery behavior of SQS Standard queues.

question: A document processing application uses SQS to queue PDF files for text extraction. Each PDF takes between 30 seconds and 15 minutes to process depending on size. The default visibility timeout is 30 seconds. Large PDFs frequently appear back in the queue before processing completes causing duplicate work. Which solution prevents messages from becoming visible while still being processed?
options: A. Set the queue visibility timeout to 15 minutes to match the maximum processing time ensuring no message becomes visible again before even the largest PDF is fully processed
B. Use the ChangeMessageVisibility API in the processing application to extend the visibility timeout periodically as processing continues allowing dynamic timeout adjustment per message
C. Configure the SQS queue as a FIFO queue which guarantees that each message is delivered once and processed in order eliminating the possibility of duplicate processing regardless of timeout
D. Implement a message deduplication check using the SQS message ID stored in ElastiCache so that if a message reappears the consumer can skip it without reprocessing the PDF document
explanation: Use the ChangeMessageVisibility API in the processing a is correct because changeMessageVisibility allows the consumer to dynamically extend the visibility timeout while processing is in progress accommodating variable processing times without setting an unnecessarily long default timeout. Set the queue visibility timeout to 15 minutes to match is incorrect because a 15-minute timeout means messages that fail processing in the first 30 seconds take 15 minutes to become available for retry wasting time for the majority of smaller PDFs that process quickly.

question: A notification system sends millions of push notifications per day using an SQS queue. Some notifications fail due to invalid device tokens. Failed notifications should be investigated but not retried more than 3 times to avoid wasting resources. How should the system handle persistently failing messages?
options: A. Configure the SQS queue with a delivery delay of 10 minutes so that failed messages are not immediately retried giving the system time to recover from transient errors before the next attempt
B. Implement application-level retry logic that counts failures in a DynamoDB counter table and deletes messages from the queue after 3 failed attempts to prevent further processing
C. Set the message retention period to a short duration like 5 minutes so that messages that fail processing are automatically deleted from the queue before they can accumulate
D. Configure a dead-letter queue with a maxReceiveCount of 3 so messages that fail processing 3 times are automatically moved to the DLQ for investigation without further retry attempts
explanation: Configure the SQS queue is correct because an SQS dead-letter queue with maxReceiveCount of 3 automatically redirects messages that fail processing 3 times to a separate queue for investigation without additional application logic. Implement application-level retry logic is incorrect because maintaining separate failure counters in DynamoDB adds complexity and the application must handle the counting deletion and investigation logic manually. Set the message retention period to a short duration li is incorrect because short retention periods would delete all messages including those not yet processed causing notification loss.

question: A financial trading platform uses SQS FIFO queues to process trade orders. Each trade must be processed in the exact order it was submitted for each stock symbol. The system handles 200 unique stock symbols with trades arriving at varying rates per symbol. Which FIFO queue configuration ensures per-symbol ordering while maximizing throughput?
options: A. Create 200 separate FIFO queues one per stock symbol and have each consumer poll its dedicated queue to maintain strict ordering while processing different symbols in parallel
B. Use an SQS Standard queue with sequence numbers embedded in the message body and implement application-level reordering using a buffer that sorts messages by sequence number before processing
C. Use a single FIFO queue with a single message group ID for all trades to ensure global ordering across all stock symbols and scale consumers horizontally to increase throughput

D. Use a single FIFO queue with the stock symbol as the message group ID which ensures messages for each symbol are processed in order while allowing messages for different symbols to be processed in parallel
explanation: Use an SQS Standard queue is correct because using the stock symbol as the message group ID in a FIFO queue ensures strict ordering within each symbol group while allowing parallel processing across different symbols maximizing throughput. Create 200 separate FIFO queues one per stock symbol is incorrect because managing 200 separate queues adds significant operational overhead and does not scale well if more symbols are added. Use a single FIFO queue is incorrect because a single message group ID forces sequential processing of all trades across all symbols creating a bottleneck that limits throughput.

question: A data pipeline processes sensor data from manufacturing equipment. The SQS queue currently has a backlog of 100000 messages and the 8 consumer instances cannot keep up with the incoming message rate. The company needs to clear the backlog and maintain real-time processing going forward. Which scaling approach addresses both the backlog and ongoing throughput?
options: A. Increase the number of consumer instances to 32 by updating the Auto Scaling group desired capacity and configure a target tracking scaling policy based on the ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible metric
B. Replace the SQS queue with Amazon Kinesis Data Streams which provides higher throughput for streaming data and can process the backlog faster using parallel shard processing with enhanced fan-out
C. Increase the message batch size from 1 to 10 in the consumer application and enable SQS long polling with a 20 second wait time to reduce the number of API calls and improve processing efficiency
D. Purge the queue to clear the backlog immediately and add more consumer instances to handle the ongoing message rate preventing future backlogs from accumulating in the queue
explanation: Increase the number of consumer instances to 32 by upda is correct because immediately increasing to 32 consumers clears the backlog and target tracking on queue depth automatically adjusts capacity to match the ongoing message rate ensuring real-time processing. Replace the SQS queue is incorrect because migrating from SQS to Kinesis is a significant architectural change that does not address the immediate backlog problem and Kinesis requires different consumer patterns.

question: A ticket booking system uses SQS to queue reservation requests. During a popular concert ticket release thousands of duplicate requests are submitted by users refreshing their browsers. The development team wants to prevent duplicate reservations without adding complex deduplication logic to the consumer. Which SQS feature handles message deduplication natively?
options: A. Configure the SQS queue with a delay queue setting of 5 minutes so duplicate submissions within the delay window are merged into a single message by the queue before delivery to consumers
B. Configure the SQS Standard queue with a long message retention period and use the message deduplication ID attribute available on Standard queues to filter duplicates at the queue level
C. Set a short visibility timeout on the SQS queue so that duplicate messages are quickly made visible and consumed by the same consumer which can then discard them based on the message attributes
D. Enable content-based deduplication on an SQS FIFO queue which uses a SHA-256 hash of the message body to automatically reject duplicate messages within the 5-minute deduplication interval
explanation: Configure the SQS queue is correct because SQS FIFO queues support content-based deduplication which automatically rejects messages with identical content within a 5-minute window eliminating duplicates without consumer-side logic. Configure the SQS Standard queue is incorrect because SQS Standard queues do not have a deduplication ID feature as message deduplication is only available on FIFO queues. Set a short visibility timeout on the SQS queue so is incorrect because visibility timeout controls how long a message is hidden from other consumers after being received and does not address deduplication of identical messages.

question: A video transcoding service uses SQS to queue transcoding jobs. Each job message contains a reference to a large video file in S3 along with transcoding parameters. The company wants to add priority processing where premium customer jobs are transcoded before free-tier customer jobs even if free-tier jobs were submitted first. Which architecture implements priority-based processing?
options: A. Use a single SQS queue and add a priority attribute to each message then configure the consumer to sort messages by priority before processing so that premium jobs are handled first

B. Create two separate SQS queues one for premium jobs and one for free-tier jobs and configure the consumer to always poll the premium queue first and only process free-tier jobs when the premium queue is empty
C. Use an SQS FIFO queue with premium jobs assigned to a message group with a higher numeric ID since FIFO queues process message groups in alphabetical order of their group ID
D. Configure the SQS queue with a shorter delivery delay for premium messages and a longer delay for free-tier messages so premium jobs become available for processing sooner after submission
explanation: Create two separate SQS queues one for premium jobs is correct because using separate queues for different priorities allows the consumer to implement priority polling where the premium queue is checked first and free-tier jobs are processed only when premium work is unavailable. Use a single SQS queue is incorrect because SQS does not support message sorting or priority-based delivery and consumers receive messages in no guaranteed order from Standard queues.

question: A healthcare company processes patient lab results using an SQS queue. Each message contains sensitive patient data that must be encrypted in transit and at rest. The company security policy requires that encryption keys are managed by the company and key usage is audited. Which SQS encryption configuration meets these requirements?
options: A. Enable SQS server-side encryption using an AWS managed KMS key (aws/sqs) which provides encryption at rest with automatic key rotation and CloudTrail logging of key usage
B. Implement client-side encryption in the producer application using a company-managed encryption key stored in AWS Secrets Manager and decrypt messages in the consumer application before processing
C. Enable SQS server-side encryption using a customer managed KMS key which provides encryption at rest with company-controlled key policies rotation schedules and CloudTrail audit logging
D. Configure the SQS queue access policy to require HTTPS connections for all API calls which provides encryption in transit and rely on the default SQS encryption for data at rest protection
explanation: Enable SQS server-side encryption is correct because a customer managed KMS key gives the company full control over the encryption key policy and rotation schedule while CloudTrail automatically logs all key usage events satisfying both the management and audit requirements. Enable SQS server-side encryption is incorrect because AWS managed keys do not allow the company to manage key policies or rotation schedules as those are controlled by AWS.

question: A logistics company uses an SQS queue to process shipping label generation requests. The consumer application runs on EC2 instances and each instance processes messages sequentially. During the holiday season the queue depth grows to over 500000 messages. Adding more EC2 instances does not reduce the backlog proportionally because each instance spends most of its time waiting for external API responses from the shipping carrier. Which architectural change improves processing throughput most effectively?
options: A. Enable SQS long polling with a 20-second wait time and increase the EC2 instance count to 100 to ensure enough instances are available to pull messages as soon as they arrive in the queue

B. Increase the SQS message batch size to 10 and implement multi-threaded processing in each EC2 instance so that each instance handles 10 messages concurrently instead of processing them sequentially
C. Switch from SQS Standard to SQS FIFO queue which provides higher throughput through message group-based parallel processing and ensures each message is processed exactly once without duplicates

D. Replace the EC2-based consumers with AWS Lambda functions triggered by SQS which automatically scales the number of concurrent executions based on queue depth handling thousands of messages in parallel
explanation: Enable SQS long polling with a 20-second wait time is correct because lambda scales automatically with queue depth and each invocation runs independently so thousands of shipping label requests can be processed in parallel while waiting for carrier API responses without blocking each other. Increase the SQS message batch size to 10 is incorrect because while multi-threading helps it still requires manual capacity management and EC2 instances have limited concurrent connection capacity compared to Lambda scaling.

question: A gaming company stores player profiles in DynamoDB with a partition key of player_id. During a tournament the top 100 players generate 80 percent of all read traffic as spectators view their profiles. This creates a hot partition problem causing throttling. The player profiles are updated every 5 minutes. Which solution resolves the hot partition issue?
options: A. Create a global secondary index on a more evenly distributed attribute like player_region to spread read traffic across partitions and query the GSI instead of the base table for profile lookups
B. Increase the provisioned read capacity units on the table by 10x to ensure enough throughput is available across all partitions including those handling the top player profiles during tournaments
C. Add a random suffix to the player_id partition key to distribute hot player records across multiple partitions and modify the application to query all possible suffixed keys and merge results
D. Enable DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) as a read-through cache in front of the table to serve repeated reads for popular player profiles from the in-memory cache reducing the load on hot partitions
explanation: Create a global secondary index on a more evenly distri is correct because DAX provides microsecond read latency from an in-memory cache and since player profiles are only updated every 5 minutes the cache can serve the vast majority of spectator reads without hitting the DynamoDB table. Increase the provisioned read capacity units on the tab is incorrect because increasing read capacity units distributes throughput across partitions but does not solve the hot key problem as all reads for a specific player_id still hit the same partition.

question: A logistics company uses DynamoDB to track package delivery status. The table has a partition key of tracking_number and a sort key of status_timestamp. The company needs to find all packages currently in transit across all tracking numbers. Scanning the entire table is too slow and expensive. Which DynamoDB feature enables efficient querying across all partition keys for a specific status?
options: A. Use DynamoDB PartiQL to run SQL-like SELECT queries with a WHERE clause on delivery_status which internally optimizes the query to avoid a full table scan on large tables
B. Create a local secondary index on the delivery_status attribute which allows querying within each partition key for a specific status and aggregate results across all tracking numbers
C. Create a global secondary index with delivery_status as the partition key and status_timestamp as the sort key to enable direct queries for all packages with a specific delivery status
D. Implement a sparse index by creating a separate DynamoDB table that only stores records for in-transit packages and synchronize it with the main table using DynamoDB Streams and Lambda
explanation: Use DynamoDB PartiQL to run SQL-like SELECT queries is correct because a GSI with delivery_status as the partition key allows direct queries for all items with a specific status like in-transit across all partition keys without scanning the base table. Create a local secondary index on the delivery_status a is incorrect because local secondary indexes only allow alternative sort keys within the same partition key so you would still need to query each tracking_number partition separately.

question: An e-commerce platform uses DynamoDB for its shopping cart. The cart data must survive any single AZ failure and be readable immediately after writes. Cart items are frequently added and removed requiring strong consistency. The company also needs to be able to recover from accidental data deletion within the last 24 hours. Which DynamoDB configuration meets all these requirements?
options: A. Use DynamoDB global tables with two Regions for cross-Region redundancy and enable point-in-time recovery for the 24-hour recovery requirement with eventually consistent reads for cart operations

B. Use a standard DynamoDB table with strongly consistent reads and enable point-in-time recovery which allows restoration to any point within the last 35 days and DynamoDB automatically replicates across three AZs
C. Use DynamoDB with on-demand capacity mode and configure DynamoDB Streams to replicate cart changes to a backup table in another Region and use the backup table for point-in-time recovery
D. Use DynamoDB with provisioned capacity and daily on-demand backups configured through AWS Backup with a 24-hour backup frequency to provide recovery from accidental deletions
explanation: Use a standard DynamoDB table is correct because dynamoDB natively replicates across three AZs providing AZ-level fault tolerance and strongly consistent reads ensure immediate read-after-write consistency while point-in-time recovery enables restoration to any second within the last 35 days. Use DynamoDB global tables is incorrect because global tables use eventually consistent cross-Region replication and the question requires strong consistency which is not available for cross-Region reads. Use DynamoDB with on-demand capacity mode is incorrect because streams-based replication to a backup table adds complexity and does not provide true point-in-time recovery to any second within a time window.

question: A ride-sharing company stores ride request data in DynamoDB. The application makes multiple writes to update ride status from requested to matched to in-progress to completed. A bug causes the application to occasionally skip the matched status and write in-progress directly. The company wants to enforce that status transitions follow the correct sequence. Which DynamoDB feature prevents invalid status transitions?
options: A. Use DynamoDB Streams to capture status changes and trigger a Lambda function that validates the transition sequence and reverts invalid updates by writing the previous status back to the table
B. Implement a DynamoDB trigger using EventBridge Pipes that evaluates status transition rules and rejects invalid transitions by filtering events before they reach the downstream processing pipeline
C. Use DynamoDB transactions to group the status read and update into an atomic operation that locks the item during the transition preventing concurrent modifications from corrupting the sequence

D. Use DynamoDB conditional writes with a condition expression that checks the current status value before allowing the update ensuring that in-progress can only be written when the current status is matched
explanation: Implement a DynamoDB trigger is correct because conditional writes with a condition expression verify the current attribute value before applying the update so the write to in-progress only succeeds if the current status is matched preventing invalid transitions at the database level. Use DynamoDB Streams to capture status changes is incorrect because streams-based validation happens after the write has already been applied and reverting creates a window of inconsistent data. Use DynamoDB transactions to group the status read is incorrect because transactions provide atomicity across multiple items but do not inherently validate business logic like status transition sequences.

question: A content management system stores article data in DynamoDB. Articles have a partition key of article_id and attributes including title body author and published_date. The application needs to retrieve only the title and author for a list page without fetching the large body attribute. Currently each query returns the full item including the body. Which approach reduces the read cost and latency?
options: A. Create a global secondary index that includes only the title and author attributes as projected attributes and query the GSI for list pages instead of the base table to return smaller items
B. Split the article data into two separate DynamoDB tables one for metadata containing title and author and another for content containing the body then join results in the application when needed

C. Use a projection expression in the GetItem or Query API call to specify that only the title and author attributes should be returned which reduces the data transferred but still reads the full item
D. Compress the body attribute using gzip before storing it in DynamoDB to reduce the item size and read capacity consumption when the full item is fetched for list page operations
explanation: Create a global secondary index is correct because a GSI with only title and author projected stores smaller items than the base table so queries against the GSI consume fewer read capacity units and return data faster for list page operations. Split the article data into two separate DynamoDB table is incorrect because splitting into two tables adds application complexity for join operations and does not leverage DynamoDB native projection capabilities.

question: A financial services company uses DynamoDB to store account balance data. The application must update account balances atomically when transferring funds between two accounts. If the debit succeeds but the credit fails the system must roll back the debit. Which DynamoDB feature ensures atomic multi-item operations?
options: A. Use DynamoDB BatchWriteItem to write both the debit and credit in a single batch request which ensures either both writes succeed or both fail atomically across multiple items
B. Use DynamoDB TransactWriteItems to perform the debit and credit as a single ACID transaction which automatically rolls back all changes if any individual operation in the transaction fails
C. Implement optimistic locking using a version number attribute on each account item and retry the entire transfer operation if a version conflict is detected during either the debit or credit write
D. Use DynamoDB Streams to trigger a Lambda function that processes the credit after the debit succeeds and implements compensation logic to reverse the debit if the credit fails asynchronously
explanation: Use DynamoDB TransactWriteItems to perform the debit is correct because transactWriteItems provides ACID transaction support across multiple items ensuring that either both the debit and credit succeed or neither is applied with automatic rollback on failure. Use DynamoDB BatchWriteItem to write both the debit is incorrect because batchWriteItem does not provide atomicity across items and individual operations in the batch can succeed or fail independently. Implement optimistic locking is incorrect because optimistic locking prevents concurrent modifications but does not provide atomicity across two separate item updates in a fund transfer scenario.

question: A social media application uses DynamoDB to store user posts. The table uses user_id as the partition key and post_timestamp as the sort key. The company wants to display a global trending feed showing the most recent posts across all users sorted by timestamp. Which approach enables this cross-partition query efficiently?
options: A. Perform a full table scan with a filter expression on post_timestamp to find recent posts and sort the results in the application layer to create the trending feed from all users
B. Create a global secondary index with a fixed partition key value like GLOBAL_FEED and post_timestamp as the sort key then write each post to both the base table and the GSI partition
C. Use DynamoDB Streams to capture new posts and write them to a separate trending feed table with a date-based partition key and post_timestamp sort key that supports efficient recent post queries
D. Create a local secondary index with post_timestamp as an alternate sort key to enable timestamp-based queries across all users without the overhead of a global secondary index
explanation: Use DynamoDB Streams to capture new posts is correct because a separate table with a date-based partition key distributes the load across partitions while enabling efficient recent post queries and DynamoDB Streams keeps it synchronized with the main table automatically. Perform a full table scan is incorrect because full table scans are expensive and slow especially as the table grows and do not scale for a real-time trending feed.

question: A multinational company needs to provide low-latency access to user preference data for applications running in us-east-1 eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1. Users primarily read their preferences but can update them from any Region. Updates must be available in all Regions within seconds. Which DynamoDB deployment meets these requirements?
options: A. Deploy separate DynamoDB tables in each Region and use a custom replication solution with SQS queues to synchronize changes between tables with eventual consistency within 5 seconds
B. Deploy DynamoDB tables in each Region with cross-Region read replicas configured through DynamoDB Streams and Lambda that replicate changes from a designated primary Region to the other two
C. Deploy a single DynamoDB table in us-east-1 and configure DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) clusters in each Region to cache reads locally and route writes back to the us-east-1 table through VPC peering
D. Deploy a single DynamoDB table in us-east-1 and use DynamoDB global tables to automatically replicate the table to eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-1 with multi-Region active-active write capability
explanation: Deploy DynamoDB tables in each Region is correct because dynamoDB global tables provide fully managed multi-Region replication with active-active write capability in all Regions and changes are typically replicated within seconds meeting the low-latency and multi-Region update requirements. Deploy separate DynamoDB tables in each Region is incorrect because building a custom replication solution with SQS adds operational complexity and is harder to maintain than the managed global tables feature.

question: A web application uses ElastiCache for Redis as a session store. When a Redis node fails all user sessions are lost forcing users to log in again. The company wants to ensure sessions survive individual node failures without impact to users. Which ElastiCache configuration provides session persistence during node failures?
options: A. Enable Redis cluster mode with multiple shards and at least one replica per shard so that if a primary node fails the replica is automatically promoted and sessions stored on that shard remain available
B. Configure ElastiCache with a single Redis node and enable Redis RDB snapshots every minute so that if the node fails the most recent snapshot can restore sessions within the last 60 seconds
C. Deploy two standalone ElastiCache Redis nodes and implement client-side session replication that writes sessions to both nodes simultaneously so either node can serve as a backup for the other
D. Switch from ElastiCache to DynamoDB for session storage since DynamoDB provides built-in high availability across three AZs without requiring any replication configuration or failover management
explanation: Enable Redis cluster mode with multiple shards is correct because redis cluster mode with replicas provides automatic failover where the replica is promoted to primary when a node fails and all session data on that shard remains available since replicas maintain a synchronized copy. Configure ElastiCache with a single Redis node is incorrect because RDB snapshots have a gap of up to 60 seconds of data loss and restoring from a snapshot requires launching a new node which takes minutes during which sessions are unavailable.

question: An e-commerce company uses ElastiCache for Redis to cache product catalog data. The cache hit rate is 95 percent but during cache misses the application queries the RDS database which adds 200ms latency. During peak events when new products are launched the cache miss rate increases to 40 percent causing database overload. Which caching strategy reduces the impact of cache misses during product launches?
options: A. Increase the ElastiCache Redis cluster size by adding more nodes to distribute the cache across more memory reducing evictions and increasing the overall cache hit rate during peak events
B. Switch from a lazy loading cache strategy to a write-through strategy where every database write also updates the cache ensuring the cache always contains the latest product data
C. Implement cache warming by pre-loading new product data into the Redis cache before the product launch event so that the cache already contains the new product data when traffic arrives
D. Configure ElastiCache to use an LFU eviction policy instead of the default LRU policy so that frequently accessed products remain in cache longer even when new products are added to the catalog
explanation: Switch from a lazy loading cache strategy to a write-th is correct because cache warming pre-populates the Redis cache with new product data before the launch event eliminating the initial cache misses that cause database overload when many users request new products simultaneously. Increase the ElastiCache Redis cluster size by adding m is incorrect because adding more nodes helps with existing data distribution but does not address the fundamental issue of new product data not being in the cache at launch time.

question: A real-time analytics dashboard queries ElastiCache for Redis to display metrics. The dashboard serves 5000 concurrent users each refreshing data every 10 seconds. The single Redis primary node is approaching its maximum connection limit of 65000. Adding more dashboard users will exceed this limit. Which solution scales the connection capacity?
options: A. Enable Redis cluster mode and add more shards to distribute connections across multiple primary nodes each with its own 65000 connection limit for horizontal connection scaling
B. Add read replicas to the ElastiCache cluster and configure the dashboard application to direct read queries to the replica endpoints distributing connections across the primary and replicas
C. Increase the maxclients parameter in the Redis configuration to 130000 to double the connection limit on the single primary node and upgrade to a larger instance type to handle the memory overhead
D. Place an Application Load Balancer in front of the ElastiCache Redis cluster to distribute incoming connections across multiple Redis nodes and manage connection pooling at the load balancer level
explanation: Add read replicas to the ElastiCache cluster is correct because read replicas accept read connections independently of the primary node and since the dashboard only reads data distributing connections across replicas scales the total connection capacity proportionally. Enable Redis cluster mode is incorrect because cluster mode shards data across nodes which helps with data distribution but connections for a specific key still go to the shard owning that key and does not proportionally increase total connection capacity for read-heavy workloads.

question: A SaaS application caches tenant-specific configuration data in ElastiCache for Memcached. When the Memcached cluster scales out by adding nodes the consistent hashing redistributes some keys to new nodes causing cache misses for affected tenants. The company wants to minimize the impact of cluster scaling on cache hit rates. Which approach reduces cache misses during scaling events?
options: A. Configure the Memcached client to use a fixed hash ring that does not change when nodes are added so that key-to-node mapping remains stable during scaling events and no keys are redistributed

B. Implement application-level cache fallback that queries the database when a cache miss occurs and repopulates the cache entry ensuring tenants experience only a single cache miss per key during scaling

C. Pre-warm the new Memcached nodes by copying all configuration data from existing nodes before adding them to the cluster so the new nodes already have the cached data when they start serving requests
D. Switch from Memcached to Redis cluster mode which uses hash slots instead of consistent hashing and supports online resharding that migrates data between nodes during scaling without cache loss
explanation: Configure the Memcached client to use a fixed hash ring is correct because redis cluster mode uses hash slot-based distribution and supports online resharding that automatically migrates keys between nodes during scaling operations preserving cached data and avoiding cache misses. Implement application-level cache fallback is incorrect because while database fallback handles individual misses it does not reduce the overall miss rate during scaling which can overwhelm the database when many keys are redistributed simultaneously.

question: A company runs a leaderboard service that uses ElastiCache for Redis sorted sets to maintain real-time player rankings. The leaderboard updates receive 10000 writes per second and 50000 reads per second. The single Redis node is reaching CPU limits. The company cannot partition the leaderboard across shards because sorted set operations require all data on a single node. Which scaling approach addresses the CPU bottleneck?
options: A. Upgrade the ElastiCache Redis node to a larger instance type with more CPU cores and memory to handle the combined read and write workload on a single node with more processing capacity
B. Add multiple read replicas and route the 50000 reads per second to the replicas while keeping all 10000 writes per second on the primary node to reduce the primary CPU load by 80 percent
C. Enable Redis cluster mode with multiple shards and use a hash tag in the sorted set key to ensure all leaderboard data resides on a single shard while still benefiting from cluster infrastructure
D. Implement a write buffer using SQS that batches leaderboard updates and applies them in bulk to Redis every second reducing the total number of Redis write operations from 10000 to 1 per second
explanation: Add multiple read replicas is correct because read replicas handle the 50000 reads per second independently of the primary node reducing the primary CPU load to only the 10000 writes per second which is manageable on a single appropriately sized node. Upgrade the ElastiCache Redis node to a larger instance is incorrect because vertical scaling has limits and Redis is single-threaded for command processing so larger instances provide more memory but do not proportionally increase single-threaded CPU throughput.

question: A healthcare application uses ElastiCache for Redis to cache patient lookup results. The compliance team requires that all cached data is encrypted at rest and in transit and that the encryption keys are rotated annually. Which ElastiCache configuration satisfies these compliance requirements?
options: A. Enable at-rest encryption using an AWS managed KMS key and enable in-transit encryption with TLS on the ElastiCache Redis cluster then configure key rotation manually by creating a new cluster annually
B. Enable at-rest encryption using a customer managed KMS key with automatic annual rotation enabled and enable in-transit encryption with TLS on the ElastiCache Redis replication group

C. Implement application-level encryption using a KMS key to encrypt data before writing to Redis and decrypt after reading which provides encryption at rest and rely on VPC security for transit encryption

D. Deploy ElastiCache Redis in a private subnet with security groups restricting access and enable Redis AUTH password authentication which encrypts all data flowing between the application and the cache
explanation: Enable at-rest encryption using a customer managed KMS key is correct because elastiCache supports at-rest encryption with customer managed KMS keys that can be configured for automatic annual rotation and in-transit encryption with TLS satisfies both encryption and key rotation requirements. Enable at-rest encryption using an AWS managed KMS key is incorrect because AWS managed KMS keys rotate automatically every year but do not provide the same level of key management control and manual cluster recreation for key rotation is disruptive and unnecessary.

question: A news website uses ElastiCache for Redis to cache article pages. During a breaking news event a cached article expires and thousands of concurrent requests simultaneously query the database to regenerate the cache entry causing a cache stampede that overwhelms the database. Which caching pattern prevents cache stampede scenarios?
options: A. Implement a cache-aside pattern with a mutex lock where the first request that finds an expired entry acquires a lock regenerates the cache and releases the lock while other requests wait or serve stale data

B. Set extremely long TTL values of 24 hours on all cached articles to minimize the frequency of cache expiration events and reduce the likelihood of multiple requests hitting the database simultaneously
C. Use ElastiCache event notifications to trigger a Lambda function that pre-regenerates cached articles before they expire based on monitoring the TTL remaining on popular article cache entries
D. Configure ElastiCache to use lazy eviction instead of TTL-based expiration so articles remain in cache indefinitely until memory pressure forces eviction of the least recently used entries
explanation: Implement a cache-aside pattern is correct because a mutex lock ensures only one request regenerates the cache entry while other concurrent requests either wait for the new value or serve slightly stale data preventing the stampede of simultaneous database queries. Set extremely long TTL values of 24 hours on all cached is incorrect because long TTL values delay the problem but do not prevent it and stale content for 24 hours is unacceptable for a news website that needs fresh breaking news content.

question: A ride-sharing company uses ElastiCache for Redis to store driver location data that is updated every second. The company is expanding to a new Region and needs the location data to be available in both Regions for cross-Region ride matching. The data must be consistent within 2 seconds across Regions. Which approach provides cross-Region Redis data availability?
options: A. Deploy separate ElastiCache clusters in each Region and use a Lambda function triggered by Redis keyspace notifications to synchronize location updates between the two clusters across Regions
B. Implement a custom replication solution using DynamoDB Streams that captures location updates and writes them to a DynamoDB global table which replicates to the secondary Region within 1 second
C. Use ElastiCache Global Datastore for Redis which provides fully managed cross-Region replication with sub-second replication lag between the primary and secondary clusters in different Regions
D. Use Amazon MemoryDB for Redis instead of ElastiCache which provides built-in cross-Region replication with strong consistency guarantees and sub-second replication for all write operations
explanation: Deploy separate ElastiCache clusters in each Region is correct because elastiCache Global Datastore provides fully managed cross-Region replication with typically sub-second replication lag meeting the 2-second consistency requirement without custom implementation. Implement a custom replication solution is incorrect because adding DynamoDB as an intermediary introduces additional latency and complexity when ElastiCache Global Datastore provides direct Redis-to-Redis cross-Region replication. Use ElastiCache Global Datastore for Redis is incorrect because custom Lambda-based synchronization introduces additional latency reliability concerns and operational overhead compared to managed cross-Region replication.

question: A content management company runs a WordPress application on multiple EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer. Each instance stores uploaded media files on its local EBS volume causing inconsistencies when users see different content depending on which instance serves their request. Which storage solution ensures all instances serve the same media files?
options: A. Configure an S3 bucket for media storage and install a WordPress plugin that redirects media uploads and retrievals to S3 instead of the local filesystem using the S3 API for all file operations
B. Set up an rsync cron job on each instance to synchronize the local media directory with all other instances every 30 seconds ensuring eventual consistency across the fleet of web servers
C. Configure EBS Multi-Attach on an io2 volume and attach it to all EC2 instances so they share the same block storage volume for media files with simultaneous read and write access
D. Create an Amazon EFS file system and mount it on all EC2 instances at the WordPress upload directory so all instances read and write media files to the same shared network file system
explanation: Set up an rsync cron job on each instance to synchroniz is correct because EFS provides a POSIX-compliant shared file system that can be mounted simultaneously on all EC2 instances giving all instances immediate consistent access to the same media files. Configure an S3 bucket for media storage is incorrect because while S3 works for media storage WordPress plugins for S3 integration add complexity and may not support all file system operations that WordPress expects.

question: A genomics research lab uses EFS to store large sequencing datasets. The datasets are written once during the sequencing process and then read many times over the following months for analysis. After 90 days datasets are accessed only a few times per year. The lab wants to reduce EFS storage costs. Which EFS feature minimizes costs for this access pattern?
options: A. Create a lifecycle policy that transitions files to EFS Infrequent Access storage class after 30 days of no access which provides up to 92 percent lower storage cost compared to EFS Standard
B. Move the datasets to S3 Glacier after 90 days using a scheduled Lambda function that copies files from EFS to S3 and deletes them from EFS to free up space on the file system
C. Reduce the EFS throughput mode from Bursting to Provisioned with a lower throughput value to reduce costs since the datasets are read-heavy and do not require burst performance after initial write
D. Enable EFS encryption at rest which compresses data as part of the encryption process reducing the effective storage consumed and lowering the per-GB storage cost for large datasets
explanation: Create a lifecycle policy is correct because EFS lifecycle management automatically moves files not accessed for the configured period to the Infrequent Access storage class which costs up to 92 percent less than Standard storage while maintaining transparent access when files are needed. Move the datasets to S3 Glacier after 90 days is incorrect because moving files to S3 removes them from the EFS mount point requiring application changes to access archived data and adds operational complexity with Lambda management.

question: A media processing company runs a video rendering farm on EC2 instances that read source video files from EFS. During peak rendering jobs the EFS file system throughput drops significantly causing rendering times to double. The file system uses Bursting throughput mode and has 500 GB of data stored. Which change to the EFS configuration resolves the throughput bottleneck?
options: A. Switch to EFS Provisioned Throughput mode and configure the throughput to match the peak rendering requirements ensuring consistent performance regardless of stored data volume
B. Add more data files to the EFS file system to increase the baseline throughput since Bursting mode throughput scales with the amount of data stored and 500 GB provides limited baseline throughput
C. Create multiple EFS file systems and distribute the source video files across them so that each file system provides independent throughput and the aggregate throughput meets peak requirements
D. Switch to EFS Max I/O performance mode which removes the throughput limit and allows unlimited parallel access from all EC2 instances in the rendering farm simultaneously
explanation: Switch to EFS Provisioned Throughput mode is correct because provisioned Throughput mode allows configuring a specific throughput level independent of storage size ensuring consistent performance during peak rendering without relying on burst credits. Add more data files to the EFS file system to increase is incorrect because adding unnecessary data to increase throughput wastes storage costs and is not a sustainable or architecturally sound solution. Create multiple EFS file systems is incorrect because distributing files across multiple EFS file systems adds complexity to the rendering pipeline and requires application changes to manage multiple mount points.

question: A software company uses EFS for shared development environments where developers run builds on EC2 instances. Build artifacts from one developer project occasionally overwrite files from another project in the shared directory. The company wants to isolate each developer project on the same EFS file system without creating separate file systems. Which EFS feature provides this isolation?
options: A. Create EFS access points for each developer project with unique root directories and POSIX user and group IDs to enforce file system-level isolation between projects on the same file system
B. Configure Linux file permissions and ACLs on the EFS mount point to create separate directories for each project with strict ownership and permission settings enforced by the operating system
C. Enable EFS encryption with different KMS keys for each developer project directory so that developers can only decrypt and access files encrypted with their project-specific encryption key
D. Create IAM policies for each developer that restrict EFS API access to specific directory paths within the file system using resource-based conditions in the IAM policy statements
explanation: Create EFS access points for each developer project is correct because EFS access points enforce a specific root directory and POSIX identity for each mount ensuring developers can only access their project directory and all file operations use the access point defined user and group IDs. Configure Linux file permissions is incorrect because linux file permissions require manual management on each EC2 instance and do not prevent root users from accessing other project directories.

question: A data science team stores Jupyter notebooks and model training data on EFS mounted to SageMaker notebook instances and EC2 training instances. The team wants to ensure their data survives a complete EFS file system corruption or accidental deletion. Which backup approach provides comprehensive protection for EFS data?
options: A. Enable EFS replication to create an automatically synchronized copy of the file system in another Region that can be promoted to a standalone file system if the primary becomes unavailable or corrupted
B. Create EFS-to-EFS backup using a DataSync task scheduled daily that copies the entire file system to a second EFS file system in another Availability Zone within the same Region
C. Configure AWS Backup to create daily backups of the EFS file system with a retention policy that keeps backups for 30 days allowing point-in-time restoration of the complete file system
D. Use a cron job on an EC2 instance to run a daily tar archive of the EFS mount point and upload the compressed archive to an S3 bucket with versioning enabled for backup history
explanation: Configure AWS Backup to create daily backups of the EFS is correct because AWS Backup provides native EFS backup support with configurable schedules retention policies and the ability to restore the complete file system to any backup point providing comprehensive protection. Enable EFS replication to create an automatically synch is incorrect because EFS replication protects against Regional failures but replicated corruption would also affect the replica since it synchronizes continuously.

question: A company runs a containerized web application on Amazon ECS with Fargate launch type. During peak traffic the application responds slowly because the current task count is insufficient. The company wants the number of tasks to automatically increase when CPU utilization exceeds 70 percent and decrease when it drops below 30 percent. Which configuration enables this automatic scaling?
options: A. Deploy the tasks on EC2 launch type instead of Fargate and use EC2 Auto Scaling to add more container instances when CPU exceeds 70 percent which automatically provides capacity for more tasks
B. Configure an Application Auto Scaling step scaling policy with two CloudWatch alarms one at 70 percent for scale-out and one at 30 percent for scale-in attached to the ECS service
C. Configure ECS Service Auto Scaling with a target tracking policy that targets 70 percent average CPU utilization across all tasks in the service to automatically adjust the desired task count
D. Use AWS Lambda to monitor the ECS service CloudWatch CPU metric and call the UpdateService API to increase the desired count when CPU exceeds 70 percent and decrease when it drops below 30 percent
explanation: Deploy the tasks on EC2 launch type instead of Fargate is correct because ECS Service Auto Scaling with target tracking on CPU utilization automatically adjusts task count to maintain the target percentage handling both scale-out and scale-in seamlessly with Fargate. Configure an Application Auto Scaling step scaling policy is incorrect because while step scaling works it requires manual configuration of two separate alarms and step adjustments whereas target tracking handles both directions with a single policy.

question: A microservices company runs 15 services on ECS with Fargate. Each service has its own Application Load Balancer costing the company significantly in ALB hourly charges. Most services receive fewer than 100 requests per second. The company wants to reduce load balancer costs while maintaining independent service routing. Which approach consolidates load balancing costs?
options: A. Replace all 15 ALBs with a single Network Load Balancer that routes traffic to all services based on port numbers with each service listening on a unique port for request differentiation
B. Replace all 15 ALBs with a single ALB using host-based or path-based routing rules to direct traffic to different target groups with each target group pointing to the respective ECS service
C. Remove all load balancers and use ECS Service Connect or AWS Cloud Map for service-to-service communication with Route 53 for external traffic routing directly to Fargate task IP addresses
D. Replace all 15 ALBs with a single Gateway Load Balancer that inspects traffic and routes it to the appropriate ECS service based on deep packet inspection of the request content and headers
explanation: Replace all 15 ALBs is correct because a single ALB with host-based or path-based routing rules can route traffic to 15 different target groups each associated with a different ECS service reducing the cost from 15 ALBs to 1 ALB. Replace all 15 ALBs is incorrect because requiring unique ports for each service complicates client configuration and NLB cannot perform HTTP-level routing needed for service differentiation. Remove all load balancers is incorrect because removing load balancers for external traffic means losing health-check-based routing and direct Fargate IP routing does not handle task replacement gracefully.

question: A company migrated a monolithic application to containers running on ECS. The application stores uploaded files in the container local filesystem. When a task is replaced the uploaded files are lost. The application expects a POSIX filesystem interface and cannot be modified to use object storage APIs. Which storage solution preserves files across task replacements while maintaining POSIX compatibility?
options: A. Mount an Amazon EFS file system to the ECS task using the EFS volume configuration in the task definition so all tasks share the same persistent POSIX-compliant storage that survives task replacements
B. Use an EBS volume attached to the ECS task through a bind mount configuration in the task definition providing persistent block storage that follows the task across replacements and host changes

C. Configure the ECS task to use a Docker volume with the local driver and enable volume persistence in the container agent configuration so the volume persists on the container host between task launches
D. Mount an S3 bucket as a filesystem using s3fs-fuse in the container entrypoint script providing S3-backed POSIX-compatible storage that persists independently of the container lifecycle
explanation: Mount an Amazon EFS file system to the ECS task is correct because EFS provides a POSIX-compliant shared file system that can be mounted directly in ECS task definitions and persists independently of task lifecycle allowing files to survive task replacements. Use an EBS volume attached to the ECS task through a bi is incorrect because EBS volumes in ECS with Fargate are ephemeral storage bound to the task lifecycle and do not persist across task replacements.

question: A company runs a sensitive API on ECS with Fargate that processes payment data. The security team requires that each ECS task has its own IAM role with least-privilege permissions and that tasks cannot access each other credentials even if they run on the same infrastructure. Which ECS configuration enforces this isolation?
options: A. Create a single IAM role with all necessary permissions and assign it as the ECS task execution role for all task definitions then use IAM policy conditions to restrict access based on the task ARN

B. Create individual IAM task roles for each task definition with only the specific permissions required and assign them in the task definition which provides credential isolation enforced by the Fargate platform

C. Create IAM roles for each ECS service and attach them to the ECS cluster capacity providers so that all tasks launched by a service inherit the service-level IAM role for consistent permission management

D. Deploy each sensitive task in a separate ECS cluster with its own VPC to ensure network-level isolation of IAM credentials and prevent cross-task credential access through the instance metadata service
explanation: Create individual IAM task roles for each task definition is correct because ECS task roles assigned in the task definition provide per-task IAM credential isolation where each task gets its own temporary credentials and Fargate ensures tasks cannot access other tasks credentials. Create a single IAM role with all necessary permissions is incorrect because a single task execution role is used for container image pulling and log publishing not for application permissions and IAM conditions on task ARN do not provide the same isolation as separate task roles.

question: A company deploys a new version of its ECS service and discovers a critical bug after 20 percent of traffic has shifted to the new version. The company needs to immediately roll back to the previous version with zero downtime. Currently the company uses a rolling update deployment type. Which ECS deployment strategy enables instant rollback capability?
options: A. Switch to the ECS blue-green deployment type using AWS CodeDeploy which maintains both old and new task sets simultaneously and can instantly shift traffic back to the old version through the ALB listener rules

B. Keep using rolling updates but reduce the minimum healthy percent to 50 and increase the maximum percent to 200 so the old tasks remain running longer providing a window for manual rollback if needed

C. Create a separate ECS service for each version and use Route 53 weighted routing to shift traffic between services with the ability to instantly change weights to route all traffic back to the old version

D. Configure the ECS service to use the DAEMON scheduling strategy which ensures exactly one task per container instance and enables version rollback by reverting the task definition to the previous revision
explanation: Switch to the ECS blue-green deployment type is correct because codeDeploy blue-green deployment maintains both the old and new task sets with traffic routing controlled through ALB enabling instant rollback by shifting the listener back to the original target group with zero downtime. Keep using rolling updates but reduce the minimum healt is incorrect because rolling updates terminate old tasks as new ones become healthy so once the rollback window closes the old tasks no longer exist requiring a new deployment to restore the previous version.

question: A company runs a machine learning inference service on ECS with Fargate. Each request takes between 100ms and 30 seconds depending on model complexity. The ALB health check is configured with a 30-second timeout. During deployments the ALB marks new tasks as unhealthy because they take longer than expected to start serving requests. Which configuration change prevents healthy tasks from being incorrectly marked as unhealthy during startup?
options: A. Increase the ECS service deployment configuration minimum healthy percent to 100 percent so that the old tasks continue serving all traffic until the new tasks are fully healthy and registered

B. Configure the ALB target group health check with a longer interval and increase the healthy threshold count to give new tasks more time to pass multiple consecutive health checks before receiving traffic
C. Configure the ALB target group with a health check grace period in the ECS service definition that delays health check evaluation until the application has completed its initialization sequence
D. Change the ALB health check path to a lightweight endpoint that returns immediately without waiting for the ML model to load so that health checks pass as soon as the container web server starts
explanation: Configure the ALB target group is correct because the ECS service health check grace period delays the evaluation of ALB health checks for a specified duration after task launch giving the application time to complete initialization before being evaluated. Increase the ECS service deployment configuration minim is incorrect because minimum healthy percent controls how many old tasks remain during deployment but does not address the health check timing issue for new tasks.

question: A company runs batch data processing jobs on ECS with Fargate. Each job processes a file from S3 and writes results back to S3. Jobs take 10-60 minutes to complete. The company currently runs each job as a long-running ECS service that polls for new files. This wastes resources when no files need processing. Which approach runs jobs only when files are uploaded and terminates tasks when processing completes?
options: A. Configure S3 event notifications to send messages to an SQS queue and have the ECS service poll the queue with the service desired count set to 0 and a CloudWatch alarm on queue depth to scale up
B. Configure the ECS service to use scheduled scaling that scales to zero tasks during off-peak hours and scales up to the required count during peak hours when files are typically uploaded

C. Replace ECS with AWS Batch configured with Fargate compute environment which automatically submits and runs jobs in response to S3 upload events with automatic resource provisioning and termination
D. Configure S3 event notifications to publish to an SNS topic that triggers a Lambda function which starts an ECS standalone task using the RunTask API to process the uploaded file
explanation: Configure S3 event notifications to send messages to an is correct because S3 event notifications trigger a Lambda function that launches an ECS standalone task via RunTask which processes the file and exits automatically when complete consuming resources only during actual processing. Configure the ECS service to use scheduled scaling is incorrect because scheduled scaling is based on time not file uploads so it wastes resources during scheduled active periods without files and misses files uploaded during off-peak periods.

question: A company runs containerized workloads on ECS with EC2 launch type. The container instances frequently run out of memory causing task placement failures. The operations team manually monitors instance memory and adds capacity reactively. The company wants to automate capacity management to ensure tasks always have available resources. Which solution automates ECS capacity management?
options: A. Over-provision the EC2 Auto Scaling group with 50 percent more instances than the current peak requirement and use reserved instances to control costs while ensuring sufficient capacity is always available

B. Create a CloudWatch alarm on the MemoryReservation metric and trigger an SNS notification to the operations team when reservation exceeds 80 percent so they can add instances before capacity is exhausted
C. Switch to Fargate launch type which eliminates the need to manage container instance capacity entirely as AWS automatically provisions the exact CPU and memory required for each task on demand

D. Configure ECS cluster auto scaling with a capacity provider that automatically adjusts the number of EC2 instances based on the cluster CPU and memory reservation ensuring tasks can always be placed
explanation: Over-provision the EC2 Auto Scaling group is correct because ECS cluster auto scaling with capacity providers automatically manages the EC2 instance count based on task placement needs ensuring sufficient memory and CPU are always available without manual intervention. Create a CloudWatch alarm on the MemoryReservation metric is incorrect because SNS notifications require manual response from the operations team which does not meet the automation requirement. Switch to Fargate launch type is incorrect because while Fargate eliminates capacity management the question asks about automating EC2 launch type capacity management and switching launch types may require significant task definition changes.

question: A company runs a Kubernetes application on Amazon EKS with worker nodes in a single Availability Zone. A recent AZ outage caused complete application downtime. The company wants the EKS cluster to survive AZ failures while maintaining consistent pod scheduling. Which configuration provides AZ-level resilience?
options: A. Deploy EKS worker nodes across three AZs using a managed node group with the subnets parameter specifying subnets in each AZ and configure pod topology spread constraints to distribute pods evenly
B. Create three separate EKS clusters one in each AZ and use a Route 53 failover routing policy to direct traffic to the healthy cluster when one AZ experiences an outage

C. Deploy worker nodes in two AZs using separate Auto Scaling groups and configure Kubernetes node affinity rules to prefer scheduling pods in the primary AZ and use the secondary only during failures
D. Use AWS Outposts to extend the EKS cluster to an on-premises data center providing a backup compute environment that activates when the primary AZ in the AWS Region becomes unavailable
explanation: Deploy EKS worker nodes across three AZs is correct because managed node groups across three AZs automatically distribute worker nodes and topology spread constraints ensure pods are evenly spread so losing one AZ leaves sufficient capacity in the remaining two. Create three separate EKS clusters one in each AZ is incorrect because managing three separate EKS clusters triples operational complexity and requires synchronization of deployments configurations and state across clusters. Deploy worker nodes in two AZs is incorrect because preferring one AZ with manual failover does not provide automatic resilience and leaves the secondary AZ underutilized during normal operations.

question: A company runs a microservices application on EKS where some services are CPU-intensive and others are memory-intensive. Running all services on the same node type results in either wasted CPU or memory. The company wants to optimize resource utilization by matching workloads to appropriate instance types. Which EKS feature enables workload-specific instance type selection?
options: A. Create multiple managed node groups with different instance types and use Kubernetes node selectors or node affinity rules in pod specifications to schedule workloads on the appropriate node group
B. Use a single managed node group with the largest available instance type to ensure both CPU-intensive and memory-intensive workloads have sufficient resources on every node in the cluster
C. Configure the Kubernetes Vertical Pod Autoscaler to automatically adjust CPU and memory requests for each pod based on observed usage patterns eliminating the need for different instance types
D. Deploy all services on Fargate profiles which automatically selects the optimal CPU and memory allocation for each pod without requiring worker node management or instance type selection
explanation: Create multiple managed node groups is correct because multiple managed node groups allow different instance types optimized for different workload profiles and Kubernetes scheduling features like node selectors and affinity rules ensure each workload runs on the most appropriate instance type. Use a single managed node group is incorrect because using the largest instance type for all nodes wastes resources when smaller workloads do not fully utilize the available CPU or memory on oversized instances.

question: A company runs an EKS cluster that needs to scale the number of pods based on application-specific metrics like queue depth from SQS rather than standard CPU or memory metrics. The Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler only supports CPU and memory metrics by default. Which approach enables custom metric-based autoscaling for EKS pods?
options: A. Deploy KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) in the EKS cluster which can scale pods based on SQS queue depth and other external event sources using custom scalers without modifying the HPA
B. Create a CloudWatch alarm on the SQS ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible metric and use a Lambda function to call the Kubernetes API to update the HPA target replica count when the alarm triggers
C. Configure the EKS cluster to use Fargate profiles for the SQS consumer pods which automatically scales the number of pods based on queue depth without requiring HPA configuration or custom metrics
D. Deploy the Kubernetes Metrics Server with CloudWatch adapter and configure HPA to use a custom metric from CloudWatch that tracks SQS queue depth as an external metric source for scaling decisions
explanation: Deploy KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling) in th is correct because KEDA provides native integration with SQS and other external event sources enabling pod autoscaling based on queue depth without complex custom metric pipeline setup. Create a CloudWatch alarm on the SQS ApproximateNumberO is incorrect because using Lambda to call the Kubernetes API introduces external dependencies bypasses native Kubernetes scaling mechanisms and requires managing Kubernetes API access from Lambda.

question: A company runs stateful databases on EKS using StatefulSets with EBS-backed persistent volumes. When a pod is rescheduled to a different AZ the pod fails to start because the EBS volume cannot be attached to a node in a different AZ. The company needs stateful pods to survive node failures while maintaining data persistence. Which solution ensures stateful pod resilience across AZ failures?
options: A. Use Amazon EFS-backed persistent volumes instead of EBS which can be mounted from any AZ simultaneously allowing stateful pods to be rescheduled to any node in the cluster without volume attachment issues

B. Configure topology-aware volume provisioning with the EBS CSI driver to ensure that pods and their EBS volumes are always co-located in the same AZ and use pod anti-affinity to spread replicas across AZs
C. Replace EBS persistent volumes with instance store volumes on the EKS worker nodes which provide higher performance and are available in every AZ without cross-AZ attachment limitations
D. Disable the Kubernetes scheduler AZ-aware scheduling feature so that pods are always rescheduled within the same AZ as their original placement ensuring EBS volume compatibility with the new node
explanation: Configure topology-aware volume provisioning is correct because topology-aware provisioning ensures EBS volumes are created in the same AZ as the pod and using multiple StatefulSet replicas with pod anti-affinity across AZs provides resilience where if one AZ fails the replica in another AZ has its own EBS volume and continues serving. Use Amazon EFS-backed persistent volumes instead of EBS is incorrect because while EFS works across AZs it provides lower IOPS and higher latency than EBS which may not meet database performance requirements.
