D. Submit a support ticket to AWS requesting a capacity reservation for P3 instances in the preferred AZ so that sufficient capacity is always available when the Auto Scaling group needs to scale
explanation: Switch the Auto Scaling group to use a mixed instances is correct because a mixed instances policy allows the Auto Scaling group to launch from multiple instance types and across multiple AZs, significantly increasing the likelihood of finding available GPU capacity. Pre-purchase Reserved Instances for the maximum number is incorrect because support tickets do not provide permanent capacity guarantees, and the correct approach is to use On-Demand Capacity Reservations through the console or API.

question: A company deploys a new application version using a rolling update in its Auto Scaling group. After the update, the health check grace period expires and instances start failing ELB health checks because the new version has a bug in the health check endpoint. The Auto Scaling group enters a cycle of launching and terminating instances. How should the architect prevent this cascading failure?
options: A. Increase the ELB health check grace period to 30 minutes to give instances more time to pass health checks and prevent the Auto Scaling group from terminating instances too quickly after launch
B. Remove the ELB health check from the Auto Scaling group and rely only on EC2 status checks so that the Auto Scaling group does not terminate instances based on application-level health failures
C. Use an Auto Scaling instance refresh with a minimum healthy percentage and automatic rollback enabled so the deployment is halted and rolled back when health check failures exceed the threshold
D. Configure the Auto Scaling group to send SNS notifications on instance termination events so the operations team can manually pause the group and investigate the issue before more instances cycle
explanation: Use an Auto Scaling instance refresh is correct because instance refresh with automatic rollback monitors the health of newly launched instances during deployment and automatically reverts to the previous launch template version if failures exceed the configured threshold, preventing cascading failures. Increase the ELB health check grace period to 30 minute is incorrect because extending the grace period only delays detection of the problem and allows unhealthy instances to serve traffic longer.

question: A SaaS company wants its Auto Scaling group to scale based on the number of requests queued in an SQS queue rather than CPU utilization. Each instance can process 100 messages per minute. Which scaling configuration is most appropriate?
options: A. Create a CloudWatch alarm on the ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible metric and configure a step scaling policy that adds instances when the queue depth exceeds 500 and removes when below 100

B. Publish a custom CloudWatch metric that divides the ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible by the number of running instances and create a target tracking policy that targets this backlog-per-instance metric

C. Configure an EventBridge rule that triggers a Lambda function every minute to check the queue depth and use the UpdateAutoScalingGroup API to set the desired capacity based on the current queue size
D. Use a simple scaling policy with a cooldown of 60 seconds that adds one instance whenever the ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible metric exceeds zero, ensuring the queue is always drained quickly
explanation: Publish a custom CloudWatch metric is correct because a custom backlog-per-instance metric with target tracking allows the Auto Scaling group to scale proportionally to the queue depth relative to current capacity, which is the AWS-recommended approach for SQS-based scaling. Create a CloudWatch alarm on the ApproximateNumberOfMes is incorrect because step scaling with fixed thresholds does not account for the current number of instances and may over-scale or under-scale depending on the fleet size. Configure an EventBridge rule is incorrect because custom Lambda-based scaling adds operational complexity and may conflict with Auto Scaling policies.

question: A company uses predictive scaling for its Auto Scaling group to handle daily traffic patterns. The group currently uses On-Demand Instances exclusively. To reduce costs, the architect wants to use a combination of On-Demand and Spot Instances while maintaining a baseline capacity. Which configuration achieves this goal?
options: A. Configure the Auto Scaling group with a mixed instances policy specifying an On-Demand base capacity and a percentage split for additional capacity between On-Demand and Spot Instances across multiple types
B. Create two separate Auto Scaling groups, one with On-Demand Instances for baseline and another with Spot Instances for burst capacity, and place both behind the same Application Load Balancer
C. Use Spot Fleet instead of an Auto Scaling group and configure it with a target capacity that includes both On-Demand and Spot Instances using the lowest-price allocation strategy for all instances
D. Set up the Auto Scaling group with all Spot Instances and create a separate manually managed fleet of On-Demand Instances that are not part of the group to serve as the baseline capacity
explanation: Configure the Auto Scaling group is correct because auto Scaling mixed instances policies natively support an On-Demand base capacity with the remaining capacity split between On-Demand and Spot using multiple instance types, all managed within a single Auto Scaling group. Create two separate Auto Scaling groups is incorrect because managing two separate Auto Scaling groups adds operational complexity and makes scaling coordination between them difficult. Use Spot Fleet instead of an Auto Scaling group is incorrect because spot Fleet does not integrate with Auto Scaling group features like predictive scaling, lifecycle hooks, and instance refresh.

question: A company needs to perform initialization tasks on new EC2 instances launched by an Auto Scaling group, including registering with a configuration management system and downloading application artifacts. If initialization fails, the instance should not receive traffic. Which mechanism enforces this requirement?
options: A. Include the initialization steps in the user data script and set the health check grace period to a value longer than the maximum expected initialization time to prevent premature termination

B. Create a CloudWatch Events rule that detects EC2 instance state changes to running and triggers a Lambda function that performs the initialization and adds the instance to the target group afterward
C. Configure the ALB health check path to return a 503 status code until initialization is complete and rely on the ALB to avoid routing traffic to instances that have not yet passed the health check
D. Add a lifecycle hook on the Pending state that holds the instance in a Pending:Wait state until initialization completes and the application sends a CompleteLifecycleAction signal to proceed
explanation: Add a lifecycle hook on the Pending state is correct because lifecycle hooks hold the instance in a Pending:Wait state during which Auto Scaling will not add it to the load balancer target group. The instance only proceeds to InService after initialization succeeds and CompleteLifecycleAction is called. If initialization fails, the instance can be abandoned. Include the initialization steps in the user data script is incorrect because the health check grace period prevents termination but does not prevent the instance from being registered with the load balancer and receiving traffic during initialization.

question: A company hosts its primary website in us-east-1 and a disaster recovery copy in eu-west-1. During normal operations, all traffic should go to us-east-1. If the primary site becomes unhealthy, traffic must automatically route to eu-west-1. Which Route 53 routing configuration meets this requirement?
options: A. Create a weighted routing policy with us-east-1 weighted at 100 and eu-west-1 weighted at 0, then manually change the weights when a failover is needed during a disaster event in the primary Region

B. Create a latency-based routing policy for both Regions and rely on the lower latency of us-east-1 for most users to naturally direct traffic there, with eu-west-1 serving as a fallback for high latency
C. Create a failover routing policy with us-east-1 as the primary record and eu-west-1 as the secondary record, and associate health checks with both records to enable automatic failover on failure

D. Create a geolocation routing policy that routes all traffic from every continent to us-east-1 and configure a default record pointing to eu-west-1 that only activates when geolocation records are removed
explanation: Create a failover routing policy is correct because route 53 failover routing with health checks automatically directs traffic to the secondary record in eu-west-1 when the primary in us-east-1 fails health checks, providing automated disaster recovery. Create a weighted routing policy is incorrect because a weight of zero means the secondary never receives traffic and manual weight changes do not provide automatic failover. Create a latency-based routing policy for both Regions is incorrect because latency-based routing may send European users to eu-west-1 even during normal operations since it is closer, and it does not provide explicit failover behavior.

question: A global retail company wants to route users to the AWS Region that provides the lowest network latency for their location. The application runs in us-east-1, eu-central-1, and ap-northeast-1 with identical deployments. Which Route 53 routing configuration achieves this goal?
options: A. Create geolocation records that map North American users to us-east-1, European users to eu-central-1, and Asian users to ap-northeast-1, with a default record for all other locations

B. Create weighted routing records with equal weights for all three Regions so traffic is distributed evenly and each user has an equal probability of being routed to the closest Region geographically
C. Create multivalue answer records for all three Regional endpoints and let the client application choose which endpoint to connect to based on its own latency measurements from the DNS response
D. Create latency-based routing records for each Region with associated health checks so Route 53 responds with the endpoint that has the lowest measured latency from the requesting resolver location
explanation: Create latency-based routing records for each Region is correct because latency-based routing uses Route 53 network latency measurements to direct users to the Region with the lowest latency from their DNS resolver location, and health checks ensure traffic avoids unhealthy endpoints. Create geolocation records is incorrect because geolocation routing assigns users based on geographic location, which may not correspond to the lowest latency path due to network routing differences. Create weighted routing records is incorrect because weighted routing distributes traffic proportionally regardless of user location, meaning users may be routed to distant Regions.

question: A company has a three-tier application with web, application, and database layers. The web layer is behind an ALB. The architect needs to create a Route 53 health check that verifies the entire application stack is functional, not just network connectivity to the ALB. Which health check configuration provides full-stack validation?
options: A. Create a Route 53 TCP health check that connects to the ALB on port 443 and verifies the connection is established, confirming the web layer is accepting network traffic on the expected port

B. Create a Route 53 HTTP health check that points to an ALB endpoint serving a dedicated health check page which queries the database and returns a 200 status code only when all layers are operational
C. Create three separate Route 53 health checks for the ALB, application server, and database, then combine them using a calculated health check that requires all three to be healthy simultaneously
D. Create a CloudWatch alarm that monitors the ALB HealthyHostCount metric and associate it with a Route 53 health check so that Route 53 considers the endpoint healthy only when hosts are registered
explanation: Create a Route 53 HTTP health check is correct because an HTTP health check that hits a dedicated status page performing end-to-end validation including database connectivity confirms the entire stack is functional before returning a healthy response. Create a Route 53 TCP health check is incorrect because a TCP check only verifies network connectivity on the port and does not validate application or database layer health. Create three separate Route 53 health checks for the ALB is incorrect because route 53 cannot directly health-check private resources like application servers and databases behind a VPC without complex workarounds.

question: A company migrates its DNS from an on-premises provider to Amazon Route 53. The company has 500 DNS records and needs to minimize the risk of DNS resolution failures during the migration. Which migration strategy is most resilient?
options: A. Export all records from the existing provider and import them into Route 53 in a single batch operation, then immediately update the domain registrar to point to Route 53 name servers
B. Create a new domain name on Route 53, copy all records to the new domain, and redirect users from the old domain to the new domain using HTTP 301 redirects until all clients update their bookmarks

C. Create all records in Route 53 first, lower the TTL values on the existing provider several days before migration, then update the domain registrar to Route 53 name servers after verifying resolution

D. Update the registrar to point to Route 53 name servers first, then begin creating records in Route 53 incrementally over several days, relying on the old provider as a fallback until migration completes
explanation: Create all records in Route 53 first is correct because creating all records in Route 53 first ensures they are ready before the name server switch. Lowering TTLs days in advance ensures clients refresh their DNS cache quickly, minimizing the window where stale records could cause resolution failures. Export all records from the existing provider is incorrect because creating a new domain and using redirects does not migrate the existing domain and breaks direct URL access.

question: A media streaming company uses Route 53 with health checks to route traffic between two ALBs in different Regions. The health checks occasionally report false failures due to transient network issues, causing unnecessary failovers. Which configuration reduces false failover events?
options: A. Disable Route 53 health checks entirely and instead rely on CloudWatch alarms monitoring the ALB TargetResponseTime metric to determine endpoint health through a calculated health check approach

B. Configure Route 53 health checks from a single Region closest to the ALB instead of the default multiple Regions, reducing the chance that geographically distant checkers report false negatives due to latency

C. Switch from HTTP health checks to TCP health checks on port 443 since TCP checks are less likely to fail due to transient application errors and only verify basic network connectivity to the endpoint

D. Increase the health check request interval from 10 seconds to 30 seconds and increase the failure threshold from 3 to 5 so that more consecutive failures are required before marking the endpoint unhealthy
explanation: Increase the health check request interval from 10 seco is correct because increasing the failure threshold means more consecutive failed checks are required before marking an endpoint unhealthy, filtering out transient network issues that resolve within a few check intervals. Disable Route 53 health checks entirely is incorrect because removing health checks entirely and relying solely on CloudWatch alarms introduces additional latency in failover detection and is more complex to configure.

question: A company operates a multi-Region active-active architecture for its customer portal. Users in different continents should be directed to the nearest healthy Region. If both Regions are healthy, traffic should be split based on user location. Which Route 53 configuration achieves this?
options: A. Use simple routing with two A records pointing to both Regional ALBs and let the client randomly select one of the returned IP addresses for each DNS query regardless of geographic location or health status

B. Use geolocation routing with health checks so that users are routed to the geographically assigned Region, and if that Region fails its health check, Route 53 falls back to the default record pointing to the other Region

C. Use failover routing with the closer Region as primary for all users and the other Region as secondary, providing automatic failover but not location-based routing for users in different continents

D. Use weighted routing with equal weights for both Regions and manually change the weights to zero for an unhealthy Region, relying on the operations team to monitor health and adjust DNS records promptly
explanation: Use geolocation routing is correct because geolocation routing directs users to the Region assigned to their continent, and with health checks enabled, Route 53 automatically falls back to the default record if the assigned Region is unhealthy. This provides both location-based routing and automatic failover. Use simple routing is incorrect because manual weight changes do not provide automatic failover and require operations team intervention. Use failover routing is incorrect because failover routing does not split traffic by location and sends all users to the primary during normal operations.

question: A company runs a microservices application where different URL paths map to different backend services. The application requires TLS termination at the load balancer and path-based routing to direct traffic to the appropriate target groups. Which ELB type and configuration meets these requirements?
options: A. Deploy an Application Load Balancer with HTTPS listeners and configure listener rules with path-based conditions to route requests to the appropriate target group for each microservice
B. Deploy a Network Load Balancer with TLS listeners and configure path-based routing rules to forward requests to different target groups based on the URL path in the incoming request
C. Deploy a Classic Load Balancer with HTTPS listeners and configure backend server authentication with separate port mappings for each microservice path to achieve path-based routing
D. Deploy a Gateway Load Balancer with TLS inspection enabled and configure routing rules based on URL paths to direct traffic to different virtual appliance target groups for each microservice
explanation: Deploy an Application Load Balancer with HTTPS listeners is correct because application Load Balancers operate at Layer 7 and natively support HTTPS termination and path-based routing rules that direct requests to different target groups based on URL path patterns. Deploy a Network Load Balancer with TLS listeners is incorrect because network Load Balancers operate at Layer 4 and do not inspect HTTP paths, so path-based routing is not supported. Deploy a Classic Load Balancer with HTTPS listeners is incorrect because gateway Load Balancers are designed for inline network appliance deployments and do not support HTTP path-based routing.

question: A financial trading application requires ultra-low latency TCP connections to backend servers with static IP addresses that clients can add to their firewall allow lists. The application handles millions of connections per second. Which load balancer configuration meets these requirements?
options: A. Deploy an Application Load Balancer with an Elastic IP address assigned to each AZ subnet and configure TCP listeners to forward connections to the backend target group at Layer 7
B. Deploy a Classic Load Balancer in TCP mode and request a static IP address from AWS Support, then configure the backend instances in the load balancer registration for TCP pass-through
C. Deploy a Network Load Balancer with Elastic IP addresses assigned to each AZ and configure TCP listeners that forward traffic at Layer 4 with minimal latency to the backend target group

D. Deploy an Application Load Balancer behind AWS Global Accelerator to obtain static IP addresses, and configure HTTPS listeners with TCP pass-through to the backend instances for low-latency connections
explanation: Deploy a Network Load Balancer is correct because network Load Balancers support static Elastic IP addresses per AZ, operate at Layer 4 for ultra-low latency TCP connections, and can handle millions of connections per second. Deploy an Application Load Balancer is incorrect because application Load Balancers do not support Elastic IP assignment and operate at Layer 7, adding latency compared to Layer 4. Deploy a Classic Load Balancer in TCP mode is incorrect because classic Load Balancers do not support static IP addresses and requesting them from Support is not a standard feature.

question: A company uses an ALB for its web application. After deploying a new version, some users report seeing inconsistent behavior because their requests alternate between old and new backend instances. The application stores session state on each instance. Which ALB feature resolves this issue?
options: A. Enable cross-zone load balancing on the ALB to ensure traffic is distributed evenly across all AZs, which naturally reduces the chance of a user hitting different instances across multiple requests
B. Configure the ALB to use least outstanding requests routing algorithm instead of round-robin so that each user connection is sent to the instance with the fewest active connections at that moment
C. Enable sticky sessions on the target group using application-based cookies so that the ALB routes subsequent requests from the same user to the same backend instance for session consistency
D. Enable connection draining on the target group with a timeout of 300 seconds so that existing connections are maintained on old instances while new connections are routed to updated instances
explanation: Enable sticky sessions on the target group is correct because sticky sessions bind a user to a specific backend instance using cookies, ensuring all requests in a session go to the same instance and see consistent application state. Enable cross-zone load balancing on the ALB to ensure t is incorrect because cross-zone load balancing distributes traffic evenly across AZs but does not ensure a specific user consistently reaches the same instance. Configure the ALB to use least outstanding requests rou is incorrect because least outstanding requests routing optimizes load distribution but does not provide session affinity for individual users.

question: A company runs a WebSocket-based real-time collaboration application. Users maintain long-lived connections to backend instances for document editing. When the ALB health check fails for an instance, active WebSocket connections are immediately dropped, disrupting users. Which configuration improves the user experience during instance deregistration?
options: A. Switch from an ALB to a Network Load Balancer because NLBs handle WebSocket connections more efficiently and do not terminate connections when backend instances are deregistered from the target group

B. Configure the ALB health check to use a longer interval and higher failure threshold so that failing instances remain registered longer, giving users more time to save their work before disconnection

C. Implement client-side reconnection logic that automatically reconnects to a different instance when the WebSocket connection drops, eliminating the impact of instance deregistration on the user experience
D. Increase the deregistration delay on the target group to allow sufficient time for active WebSocket connections to complete naturally before the instance is fully removed from the load balancer
explanation: Increase the deregistration delay on the target group t is correct because increasing the deregistration delay gives existing WebSocket connections time to complete gracefully while the ALB stops sending new connections to the failing instance. Switch from an ALB to a Network Load Balancer because N is incorrect because nLBs also close connections during deregistration and do not natively handle WebSocket session management better than ALBs for this scenario. Configure the ALB health check to use a longer interval is incorrect because keeping unhealthy instances registered longer means they may serve degraded responses to both existing and new users.

question: A company uses an ALB with health checks configured to verify instance health every 30 seconds. The application occasionally takes 60 seconds to fully initialize after launch. Newly launched instances are being marked unhealthy and terminated by the Auto Scaling group before they finish starting up. Which adjustment resolves this issue?
options: A. Increase the healthy threshold from 2 to 10 consecutive successful health checks so the instance must prove stability over a longer period before being considered healthy by the load balancer
B. Change the health check interval from 30 seconds to 5 seconds so the ALB detects the instance as healthy more quickly once the application finishes its 60-second initialization process
C. Disable the ELB health check on the Auto Scaling group entirely and use only EC2 status checks since the ELB health check is causing premature termination of instances during initialization
D. Increase the health check grace period on the Auto Scaling group to at least 90 seconds so the group does not act on ELB health check failures until the instance has had time to initialize
explanation: Increase the health check grace period on the Auto Scal is correct because the health check grace period tells the Auto Scaling group to ignore ELB health check failures for the specified duration after launch, allowing the instance time to fully initialize before health is evaluated. Increase the healthy threshold from 2 to 10 consecutive is incorrect because increasing the healthy threshold makes it harder for the instance to be marked healthy and does not prevent the Auto Scaling group from terminating it during initialization.

question: A company operates a multi-tenant SaaS platform where each tenant has a unique subdomain such as tenant1.example.com. All tenants share the same set of backend servers. The architect needs to route requests based on the Host header to different target groups for premium tenants while serving standard tenants from a shared pool. Which approach achieves this?
options: A. Configure ALB listener rules with host-based routing conditions that match premium tenant subdomains and forward to dedicated target groups, with a default rule directing remaining traffic to the shared pool

B. Create separate ALBs for each premium tenant with dedicated target groups and use Route 53 alias records to point each premium subdomain to its specific ALB while standard tenants share a common ALB

C. Deploy a Network Load Balancer with TLS listeners and use SNI to identify the tenant subdomain, then route traffic to different target groups based on the SSL certificate presented by the client connection

D. Use an API Gateway with custom domain names for each premium tenant and configure separate stage deployments that route to different backend integrations while standard tenants use a shared API stage
explanation: Configure ALB listener rules is correct because ALB listener rules support host-based routing conditions that match specific Host header values, allowing premium tenant subdomains to be directed to dedicated target groups while a default rule handles standard tenants. Create separate ALBs for each premium tenant is incorrect because creating separate ALBs for each premium tenant is expensive and operationally complex compared to using listener rules on a single ALB.

question: A healthcare application behind an ALB must ensure that all traffic between clients and the backend instances is encrypted end-to-end. The ALB currently terminates TLS and communicates with instances over HTTP. Which change establishes end-to-end encryption?
options: A. Switch from an ALB to a Classic Load Balancer in TCP mode so that TLS connections pass through directly to the backend instances without any termination or re-encryption at the load balancer layer
B. Enable the ALB access logs and store them in an encrypted S3 bucket to ensure all traffic metadata is encrypted at rest, which satisfies the end-to-end encryption compliance requirement fully
C. Configure the ALB target group to use HTTPS protocol and install TLS certificates on the backend instances so the ALB re-encrypts traffic before forwarding it to the instances over port 443

D. Configure the ALB to use mutual TLS authentication on the listener so that both the client and the ALB verify certificates, which ensures all traffic between client and ALB is encrypted bidirectionally
explanation: Configure the ALB target group to use HTTPS protocol is correct because configuring the target group protocol to HTTPS causes the ALB to establish a new TLS connection to backend instances, ensuring traffic is encrypted both between client and ALB and between ALB and instances, achieving end-to-end encryption. Switch from an ALB to a Classic Load Balancer in TCP mo is incorrect because switching to a Classic Load Balancer loses ALB features like path-based routing, and TCP pass-through mode does not allow the ALB to inspect or route based on HTTP attributes.

question: A company runs an application behind an ALB that serves both API and web traffic. API requests go to /api/* and must return JSON error responses, while web requests serve HTML pages. When a backend instance is unavailable, the ALB returns a generic 502 error. The architect wants custom error responses for each traffic type. Which solution achieves this?
options: A. Configure ALB listener rules with fixed-response actions that return appropriate JSON or HTML error messages when no healthy targets are available, using separate rules for /api/* and all other paths

B. Create a custom error page in Amazon S3 and configure the ALB to redirect all 502 errors to the S3 page, then use JavaScript on the page to detect whether the original request was for API or web content

C. Deploy a Lambda function as an ALB target that catches 502 errors from the primary target group and returns the appropriate response format based on the request path before forwarding to the client

D. Configure the ALB access logs to detect 502 errors and trigger a CloudWatch alarm that invokes a Lambda function to update the ALB listener rules dynamically with custom error responses on each failure
explanation: Configure ALB listener rules is correct because ALB fixed-response actions can return custom status codes and body content including JSON or HTML, and separate listener rules for /api/* and default paths allow different error responses for each traffic type. Create a custom error page in Amazon S3 is incorrect because aLBs cannot redirect based on specific error codes, and using JavaScript to detect the original request type is unreliable and complex.

question: A company exposes internal microservices to other teams within the same VPC using an internal ALB. The security team requires that only specific security groups can access the internal ALB. Recently, they discovered that any instance in the VPC could reach the ALB. Which approach restricts access to authorized services only?
options: A. Move the internal ALB to a dedicated VPC and create VPC peering connections only with the VPCs containing authorized services to physically isolate the load balancer from unauthorized access
B. Configure network ACLs on the ALB subnets to allow traffic only from the specific CIDR ranges of authorized service subnets and deny all other inbound traffic at the network ACL layer
C. Configure the ALB security group inbound rules to allow traffic only from the specific security groups of authorized services, denying all other inbound traffic to the internal ALB listener ports
D. Enable AWS WAF on the internal ALB and create IP-based rules that allow requests only from the IP addresses of authorized service instances while blocking all other source IP addresses
explanation: Configure the ALB security group inbound rules to allow is correct because ALB security group inbound rules can reference other security groups, allowing only instances associated with those specific security groups to access the ALB. This is the simplest and most maintainable approach. Move the internal ALB to a dedicated VPC is incorrect because creating separate VPCs and peering connections adds significant network complexity for what is a simple access control requirement.

question: A media company stores original video files in S3. Editors frequently need to recover previous versions of files that were accidentally overwritten. Currently, overwritten files cannot be retrieved. Which S3 feature enables recovery of previous file versions?
options: A. Enable S3 Object Lock in governance mode on the bucket to prevent any object from being overwritten, requiring editors to submit a request to unlock the object before uploading a new version
B. Configure S3 cross-Region replication to a backup bucket so that overwritten files are preserved as the original copy in the destination bucket even after the source file is overwritten
C. Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket to create multiple copies of each object across AWS edge locations, allowing editors to retrieve previous copies from edge caches after overwrites
D. Enable S3 versioning on the bucket so that every object overwrite creates a new version while preserving previous versions, allowing editors to retrieve any prior version of a file at any time
explanation: Enable S3 versioning on the bucket so is correct because S3 versioning preserves every version of an object, including all overwrites and deletions, allowing any previous version to be retrieved at any time. Enable S3 Object Lock in governance mode on the bucket is incorrect because object Lock prevents deletion or overwrite entirely and would block editors from updating files, which is not the desired workflow. Configure S3 cross-Region replication to a backup bucket so is incorrect because cross-Region replication replicates the latest version including overwrites, so the destination bucket would also have the overwritten file.

question: A healthcare organization must store patient records in S3 for seven years to comply with regulatory requirements. The records must not be deleted or modified by any user, including the root account, during the retention period. Which S3 configuration enforces this compliance requirement?
options: A. Enable S3 versioning and create a bucket policy that denies s3:DeleteObject and s3:PutObject actions for all principals, with a condition that checks whether the object age is less than seven years
B. Enable S3 Object Lock in compliance mode with a retention period of seven years so that no user, including the root account, can delete or overwrite objects until the retention period expires

C. Enable S3 Object Lock in governance mode with a retention period of seven years, which prevents standard users from deleting objects but allows administrators with special permissions to override if needed
D. Create an S3 lifecycle policy that transitions objects to S3 Glacier Deep Archive after 30 days and configure the Glacier vault with a vault lock policy that prevents deletion for seven years
explanation: Enable S3 Object Lock in compliance mode is correct because S3 Object Lock in compliance mode provides write-once-read-many protection that cannot be overridden by any user, including the root account, until the retention period expires. Enable S3 versioning is incorrect because bucket policies can be modified by administrators or the root account, so they do not provide immutable protection. Enable S3 Object Lock in governance mode is incorrect because governance mode allows users with the s3:BypassGovernanceRetention permission to override the retention, which does not meet the requirement of preventing all users including root from deleting objects.

question: A company stores critical business data in a single S3 bucket in us-east-1. The compliance team requires that a copy of all data be maintained in a different Region for disaster recovery. Changes to data in the primary bucket must be reflected in the secondary bucket within 15 minutes. Which solution meets these requirements?
options: A. Create a Lambda function triggered by S3 event notifications that copies each new or modified object to a bucket in us-west-2 using the S3 CopyObject API within the function execution timeout period
B. Use AWS DataSync with a scheduled task that runs every 15 minutes to synchronize the contents of the us-east-1 bucket to a bucket in us-west-2, transferring only changed or new files each run

C. Configure S3 cross-Region replication from the us-east-1 bucket to a bucket in us-west-2 with versioning enabled on both buckets, which replicates new and updated objects to the destination automatically

D. Configure S3 Same-Region Replication to a second bucket in us-east-1 and then configure cross-Region replication from that second bucket to us-west-2 to create a two-hop replication chain for redundancy
explanation: Configure S3 cross-Region replication from the us-east- is correct because S3 cross-Region replication automatically and asynchronously copies new and updated objects to a destination bucket in another Region, typically completing within 15 minutes for most objects, and is the native AWS solution for this requirement. Create a Lambda function triggered by S3 event notifica is incorrect because a custom Lambda function adds operational complexity, may encounter timeout limits for large objects, and requires error handling and retry logic.

question: A news organization stores millions of article images in S3. Images less than 30 days old are accessed frequently, images between 30 and 180 days are accessed rarely, and images older than 180 days are almost never accessed but must be retained for five years. Which lifecycle configuration minimizes storage costs?
options: A. Store all images in S3 Standard and create a lifecycle rule that deletes objects after 180 days, then use AWS Backup to create monthly snapshots that are retained for five years in a separate vault

B. Store all images in S3 Intelligent-Tiering from the start and let the automatic monitoring move objects between frequent and infrequent access tiers based on actual access patterns over the five-year retention

C. Store images in S3 Standard, transition to S3 Standard-IA after 30 days, transition to S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval after 180 days, and set the lifecycle to expire objects after five years from creation

D. Store all images in S3 One Zone-IA from the beginning since most images are infrequently accessed and the lower cost of single-AZ storage provides the best overall cost optimization for the entire lifecycle
explanation: Store images in S3 Standard is correct because it matches each access pattern to the most cost-effective storage class: Standard for frequent access in the first 30 days, Standard-IA for rare access up to 180 days, and Glacier for long-term archival, with automatic expiration after five years. Store all images in S3 Standard is incorrect because deleting objects after 180 days and relying on AWS Backup snapshots is more complex and does not provide the same ease of retrieval as S3 Glacier.

question: A software company distributes build artifacts from an S3 bucket to development teams. A developer accidentally deleted a critical artifact file and the company has no backup. The bucket does not have versioning enabled. Which preventive measure should the architect implement to protect against accidental deletions going forward?
options: A. Create a bucket policy that denies all s3:DeleteObject requests from IAM users and allows deletions only through a specific IAM role that requires approval from two administrators before use
B. Enable S3 event notifications for delete events and create a Lambda function that immediately copies the deleted object from the S3 access logs back to the bucket before the deletion takes effect
C. Schedule an AWS Backup job to take daily snapshots of the entire S3 bucket and store the backups in a separate AWS account so that deleted files can be restored from the most recent daily backup

D. Enable S3 versioning on the bucket and optionally add MFA Delete to require multi-factor authentication for permanent version deletions, allowing recovery of accidentally deleted objects from prior versions
explanation: Enable S3 versioning on the bucket is correct because enabling versioning preserves all object versions including deletions as delete markers, allowing easy recovery of accidentally deleted objects. MFA Delete adds an additional layer of protection against permanent version deletion. Create a bucket policy is incorrect because restricting deletions entirely may impede normal workflows and does not provide a recovery mechanism for objects deleted through authorized channels. Enable S3 event notifications for delete events is incorrect because S3 access logs do not contain object data and a Lambda function cannot reverse a deletion after it occurs.

question: A data analytics company runs queries against large CSV files stored in S3. Currently, the application downloads entire files to EC2 instances for processing, which is slow for files over 10 GB. The queries typically need only a subset of columns and rows. Which S3 feature reduces data transfer and speeds up query processing?
options: A. Use S3 Select to push filtering down to S3 and retrieve only the specific rows and columns needed for the query, reducing the amount of data transferred from S3 to the EC2 instance significantly

B. Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket to speed up the download of large files from S3 to EC2 instances by using CloudFront edge locations as an intermediate transfer point for faster throughput
C. Split each large CSV file into thousands of smaller files and use S3 multipart download to retrieve only the small files that contain the needed data, reducing the total amount of data downloaded
D. Move the CSV files to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval which provides faster retrieval performance for large objects compared to S3 Standard, making the download of 10 GB files complete in seconds
explanation: Use S3 Select to push filtering down to S3 is correct because S3 Select allows SQL-like filtering at the S3 level, returning only the requested rows and columns, dramatically reducing data transfer and processing time for large files. Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket to speed is incorrect because pre-splitting files adds management complexity and the application would still need to know which small files contain the relevant data.

question: A company uses S3 to store application logs from hundreds of EC2 instances. Each instance writes log files to the same S3 prefix every minute, creating thousands of PUT requests. The company reports that some log files are being lost. Which S3 behavior most likely explains this issue and how should it be resolved?
options: A. Multiple instances are writing files with identical keys at the same millisecond and the last writer wins, overwriting earlier files. Add instance ID and timestamp to each object key to ensure uniqueness

B. S3 has a request rate limit per prefix and the high volume of PUT requests is being throttled, causing some writes to fail silently. Distribute writes across multiple prefixes using a randomized naming convention

C. S3 eventually consistent reads are returning stale data that makes it appear files are missing when they actually exist. Wait longer before querying for newly written files to allow consistency to propagate

D. The S3 bucket has reached its maximum object count limit and new objects are being rejected. Create additional buckets and distribute log writes across them to stay under the per-bucket object limit
explanation: Multiple instances are writing files is correct because if multiple instances write objects with the same key name simultaneously, S3 will store only the last write, causing earlier files to appear lost. Including the instance ID and timestamp in the key ensures unique object names. S3 has a request rate limit per prefix is incorrect because S3 supports thousands of PUT requests per second per prefix and automatically scales, making throttling unlikely to cause silent data loss without returning errors.

question: A company hosts a static website on S3 with CloudFront as the CDN. The website serves users globally. During an S3 Regional outage, the entire website becomes unavailable because CloudFront cannot reach the origin. The company needs the website to remain available during S3 outages. Which architecture improves availability?
options: A. Configure CloudFront to increase its default TTL to 7 days so that all website content remains cached at edge locations for an extended period, eliminating the need to contact the S3 origin during outages
B. Switch from S3 static website hosting to hosting the website on an EC2 instance behind an ALB, which provides more resilient origin hosting than S3 for CloudFront distributions

C. Enable S3 cross-Region replication to a bucket in a second Region and configure CloudFront with an origin group containing both Regional S3 origins, with automatic failover to the secondary on errors
D. Enable S3 versioning and cross-Region replication, then configure Route 53 failover routing between the two S3 website endpoints, using the Route 53 URL as the CloudFront origin domain
explanation: Enable S3 cross-Region replication to a bucket in a sec is correct because cloudFront origin groups support automatic failover between a primary and secondary origin. If the primary S3 origin returns errors, CloudFront automatically routes requests to the secondary S3 origin in another Region. Configure CloudFront to increase its default TTL to 7 d is incorrect because increasing TTL to 7 days would serve stale content and new or recently updated pages would be unavailable during an outage.

question: A financial company needs to upload 50 TB of historical data to S3 within one week. The company has a 1 Gbps Direct Connect link that is already 70 percent utilized by production traffic. Uploading over the network would take too long and impact production. Which method transfers the data within the time constraint?
options: A. Use S3 Transfer Acceleration to speed up the upload over the existing Direct Connect link by routing traffic through CloudFront edge locations for optimized network paths to the S3 bucket
B. Create a second Direct Connect connection dedicated to data transfer and upload all 50 TB over the new connection using S3 multipart upload to maximize throughput during the transfer window
C. Compress the 50 TB dataset to approximately 10 TB and upload the compressed archive over the existing Direct Connect link during off-peak hours using scheduled S3 multipart uploads
D. Order an AWS Snowball Edge device to transfer the 50 TB of data offline, ship it to AWS for ingestion into S3, which avoids using network bandwidth and meets the one-week deadline
explanation: Order an AWS Snowball Edge device to transfer the 50 TB is correct because AWS Snowball Edge can transfer up to 80 TB of data and the entire process from ordering to data ingestion typically completes within a week, without consuming any network bandwidth. Use S3 Transfer Acceleration to speed up the upload ove is incorrect because transfer Acceleration speeds up internet-based transfers and does not help with Direct Connect, and the link is already 70 percent utilized.

question: An order processing system uses SQS standard queues to decouple the web tier from the processing tier. During peak sales events, the processing tier falls behind and orders take hours to process. The company needs to automatically scale processing capacity based on queue depth. Which solution achieves this?
options: A. Configure SQS long polling with a 20-second wait time and increase the number of messages each instance retrieves per poll from 1 to 10 so that each existing instance processes messages faster during peak periods

B. Switch from SQS standard queues to FIFO queues which process messages more efficiently due to their ordered delivery guarantee, reducing the time required to process each individual order in the queue

C. Create a CloudWatch alarm on the ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible metric and configure an Auto Scaling step scaling policy that adds EC2 processing instances when the queue depth exceeds defined thresholds

D. Create a scheduled Lambda function that runs every hour and checks the queue depth, then uses the EC2 API to manually launch additional processing instances if the queue depth exceeds a predefined threshold
explanation: Create a CloudWatch alarm on the ApproximateNumberOfMes is correct because a CloudWatch alarm on queue depth with an Auto Scaling step scaling policy automatically adds processing capacity when the queue grows, directly addressing the backlog during peak events. Configure SQS long polling with a 20-second wait time is incorrect because long polling and batch retrieval improve efficiency but do not add processing capacity, so a fixed number of instances will still fall behind during sustained peaks.

question: A company uses SQS to queue payment processing requests. Some payments fail due to temporary bank API outages. Failed payments are currently lost because the processing application deletes the message before confirming payment success. Which approach ensures failed payments are retried without losing any messages?
options: A. Increase the message retention period on the SQS queue from 4 days to 14 days so that messages remain in the queue longer, giving the bank API more time to recover before messages expire and are deleted

B. Create a duplicate SQS queue and publish every payment message to both queues simultaneously, processing from the primary queue and using the secondary queue as a backup if messages fail in the primary
C. Store a copy of every payment message in a DynamoDB table before publishing to SQS, then run a scheduled job that checks for unprocessed payments in DynamoDB and republishes them to the SQS queue

D. Configure the application to delete the message only after the payment is confirmed successful, and configure a dead-letter queue with a maximum receive count so persistently failing messages are moved aside
explanation: Configure the application to delete the message only af is correct because deleting messages only after successful processing ensures failed payments become visible again after the visibility timeout for retry. The dead-letter queue captures messages that fail repeatedly, preventing infinite retry loops while preserving them for investigation. Increase the message retention period on the SQS queue is incorrect because increasing retention does not prevent the application from deleting messages prematurely after a failed processing attempt. Create a duplicate SQS queue is incorrect because duplicate queues add complexity and do not address the root cause of premature message deletion.

question: A document management system allows users to upload files which are then processed asynchronously. The processing involves virus scanning, format conversion, and metadata extraction. Each step must complete before the next begins, and if any step fails, the entire pipeline should not proceed. Which architecture coordinates this sequential processing?
options: A. Use a single SQS queue and implement all three processing steps in a single Lambda function that executes them sequentially, deleting the message only if all three steps complete without any errors occurring

B. Create a fan-out pattern with an SNS topic that distributes each upload to three SQS queues simultaneously, with each queue feeding a separate processing step that runs independently and in parallel with others

C. Create three separate SQS queues chained together where each processing step reads from one queue, performs its work, and if successful publishes a message to the next queue in the sequence to trigger the next step

D. Use Amazon EventBridge to trigger all three processing steps as separate targets of a single rule, relying on EventBridge to manage the ordering and retry logic for each step automatically in sequence
explanation: Create three separate SQS queues chained together where is correct because chaining SQS queues ensures each step only proceeds when the previous step succeeds and publishes to the next queue. If any step fails, the message remains in its current queue for retry without triggering downstream steps. Use a single SQS queue is incorrect because a single Lambda function running all three steps creates a monolithic processor that may exceed Lambda timeout limits and does not allow independent scaling or retry of individual steps.

question: A company processes customer support tickets using an SQS FIFO queue to ensure tickets from the same customer are handled in the order they were submitted. Processing takes 2 to 5 minutes per ticket. The team notices that when one customer submits many tickets, processing of tickets from other customers is blocked. Which change resolves this issue?
options: A. Use the message group ID to assign each customer a unique group so that tickets within a customer group are ordered while tickets from different customers can be processed concurrently across message groups

B. Switch from a FIFO queue to a standard queue and implement application-level ordering using sequence numbers stored in the message body, allowing all tickets to be processed concurrently without blocking

C. Increase the number of consumer instances from one to ten so that multiple tickets can be processed simultaneously and the high-volume customer tickets do not create a bottleneck for other customers

D. Set the message deduplication scope to queue level instead of message group level so that each ticket is guaranteed unique across the entire queue and cannot block tickets in other groups from processing
explanation: Use the message group ID to assign each customer a uniq is correct because FIFO queues use message group IDs to maintain ordering within groups while allowing parallel processing across groups. Assigning each customer a unique group ID means one customer's tickets do not block others. Switch from a FIFO queue to a standard queue is incorrect because switching to a standard queue loses the ordering guarantee entirely and application-level ordering adds significant complexity.

question: A company uses SQS to decouple a web application from a backend processing service. The messages contain sensitive customer data including credit card numbers. Compliance requires that message content be encrypted at rest. Which configuration meets this requirement with the least operational overhead?
options: A. Implement client-side encryption in the producer application using the AWS Encryption SDK to encrypt messages before sending them to SQS, and decrypt in the consumer application after receiving the messages

B. Create a VPC endpoint for SQS and configure the endpoint policy to require TLS 1.2 for all connections, which encrypts messages during transit and satisfies the at-rest encryption compliance requirement

C. Store the sensitive data in an encrypted DynamoDB table and send only a reference key in the SQS message, so the consumer retrieves the sensitive data from DynamoDB using the key from the message body

D. Enable SQS server-side encryption using an AWS managed KMS key which automatically encrypts all messages at rest and decrypts them when consumed, requiring no changes to the producer or consumer applications
explanation: Enable SQS server-side encryption is correct because SQS server-side encryption with an AWS managed KMS key encrypts all messages at rest transparently without requiring any code changes in producer or consumer applications, providing the least operational overhead. Implement client-side encryption in the producer applic is incorrect because client-side encryption requires code changes in both the producer and consumer applications and the operational overhead of managing encryption keys. Create a VPC endpoint for SQS is incorrect because TLS encrypts data in transit, not at rest, so it does not meet the compliance requirement for at-rest encryption.

question: A notification system sends messages to an SQS queue for delivery to multiple downstream services. Each service needs to receive every message independently. Currently, once one service reads and deletes a message, other services cannot access it. Which architecture allows all services to receive every message?
options: A. Create separate SQS queues for each downstream service and have the producer application send a copy of every message to each queue individually, ensuring every service gets its own copy of each message

B. Change the SQS queue visibility timeout to zero seconds so that messages remain visible to all consumers simultaneously and each service can read the message without it becoming invisible to others

C. Configure SQS message attributes to include a list of target services and implement consumer-side filtering so each service only deletes the message after all services listed in the attribute have processed it
D. Use an SNS topic as the entry point and subscribe each downstream service SQS queue to the topic, so that every message published to the topic is delivered to all subscribed queues independently
explanation: Use an SNS topic as the entry point is correct because the SNS fan-out pattern publishes each message to all subscribed SQS queues independently, ensuring every downstream service receives its own copy without affecting other services. Create separate SQS queues for each downstream service is incorrect because having the producer send messages to multiple queues individually creates tight coupling between the producer and all consumers, and requires producer changes whenever a new service is added.

question: A company runs a data ingestion pipeline where producers send 50 KB JSON payloads to an SQS queue. The company plans to increase the payload size to 1 MB. SQS has a 256 KB message size limit. The architecture must continue using SQS for queuing while handling larger payloads. Which approach supports the larger payload size?
options: A. Compress the 1 MB JSON payload using gzip before sending it to SQS, reducing the size to below the 256 KB limit, and decompress it in the consumer application after receiving the message from the queue

B. Split each 1 MB message into four separate 256 KB SQS messages with sequence numbers and reassemble them in the consumer application by collecting all four parts before processing the complete payload

C. Use the Amazon SQS Extended Client Library to store the message payload in S3 and send only a reference pointer in the SQS message, allowing the consumer to retrieve the full payload from S3 automatically
D. Switch from SQS to Amazon Kinesis Data Streams which supports a maximum record size of 1 MB, eliminating the need for any workaround and allowing the producer to send the full payload directly
explanation: Use the Amazon SQS Extended Client Library to store the is correct because the SQS Extended Client Library transparently stores large payloads in S3 and places a reference in the SQS message, allowing payloads up to 2 GB while maintaining the SQS queuing pattern. Compress the 1 MB JSON payload is incorrect because gzip compression ratio varies by data content and there is no guarantee that 1 MB of JSON will compress below 256 KB.

question: A company uses SQS to buffer requests between a frontend API and a backend service. During a backend outage lasting two hours, messages accumulated in the queue. When the backend recovered, the sudden flood of accumulated messages overwhelmed the database. Which approach prevents this thundering herd problem?
options: A. Reduce the SQS visibility timeout to 5 seconds so that if a consumer fails to process a message quickly, it becomes visible to another consumer faster, distributing the load across more consumers

B. Switch to an SQS FIFO queue which processes messages one at a time in strict order, naturally limiting the throughput and preventing the backend from receiving too many messages simultaneously after recovery

C. Set the message retention period to 30 minutes so that messages accumulated during a long outage are automatically deleted, preventing the backlog from overwhelming the backend when it recovers from the failure

D. Configure the backend consumer to use SQS long polling and process messages at a controlled rate by limiting the number of concurrent consumers and the batch size per receive request to throttle throughput
explanation: Configure the backend consumer to use SQS long polling is correct because controlling the number of concurrent consumers and batch size effectively throttles the rate at which messages are processed, preventing the backend from being overwhelmed by a sudden flood of accumulated messages. Reduce the SQS visibility timeout to 5 seconds so is incorrect because reducing the visibility timeout causes more frequent retries but does not limit the overall processing rate, potentially worsening the database load.

question: A company processes image uploads through an SQS queue. Each image processing job takes between 30 seconds and 10 minutes depending on image size. The visibility timeout is set to 2 minutes. The team discovers that large images are being processed multiple times because the message becomes visible again before processing completes. Which solution prevents duplicate processing?
options: A. Set the visibility timeout to 15 minutes to accommodate the longest possible processing time, ensuring no message becomes visible again before even the largest images are fully processed by the consumer

B. Have the consumer call ChangeMessageVisibility to extend the timeout dynamically based on the estimated remaining processing time, keeping the message invisible until processing is confirmed complete

C. Switch to an SQS FIFO queue with message deduplication enabled using content-based deduplication so that identical messages are automatically rejected and cannot be processed more than once in the queue

D. Store a record of each processed message ID in a DynamoDB table and check the table before processing each message, skipping any message whose ID already exists in the table as previously processed
explanation: Have the consumer call ChangeMessageVisibility to exten is correct because dynamically extending the visibility timeout using ChangeMessageVisibility allows the consumer to keep the message hidden for exactly as long as needed, accommodating variable processing times without setting an excessively long default timeout. Set the visibility timeout to 15 minutes to accommodate is incorrect because FIFO deduplication prevents duplicate submissions within a 5-minute window but does not address the visibility timeout issue causing the same message to be received again by another consumer.

question: A social media application uses DynamoDB to store user profiles. The application experiences unpredictable traffic spikes when viral content drives millions of users to update their profiles simultaneously. The current provisioned capacity is frequently exceeded, causing throttling. Which capacity mode handles these unpredictable spikes?
options: A. Increase the provisioned read and write capacity units to the peak expected value and enable DynamoDB Auto Scaling with a target utilization of 50 percent to handle bursts above the provisioned baseline

B. Implement write sharding by appending a random suffix to the partition key, distributing writes across more partitions to prevent hot partition throttling during traffic spikes on popular user profiles

C. Switch to DynamoDB on-demand capacity mode which automatically accommodates traffic spikes by scaling read and write capacity instantly without requiring capacity planning or provisioning in advance

D. Add a DynamoDB Accelerator cluster in front of the table to cache frequently accessed profiles, reducing the number of read and write requests that reach the underlying DynamoDB table during spikes
explanation: Switch to DynamoDB on-demand capacity mode is correct because on-demand capacity mode instantly scales to handle any level of read or write traffic without requiring capacity planning, making it ideal for unpredictable workloads. Increase the provisioned read is incorrect because dynamoDB Auto Scaling adjusts capacity gradually based on utilization metrics and cannot respond fast enough to sudden viral traffic spikes. Implement write sharding by appending a random suffix t is incorrect because write sharding helps with hot partition issues but does not address the overall table-level capacity being exceeded.

question: A global e-commerce company needs to provide sub-10-millisecond read and write latency for its product catalog stored in DynamoDB. The application serves customers in North America, Europe, and Asia Pacific. Which DynamoDB feature provides low-latency access from all three regions?
options: A. Deploy DynamoDB global tables with replicas in us-east-1, eu-west-1, and ap-southeast-1 which provide active-active multi-Region replication with single-digit millisecond read and write latency locally

B. Create separate DynamoDB tables in each Region and use AWS Data Pipeline to synchronize data between them every five minutes, providing each Region with a local copy of the catalog for low-latency reads

C. Place a DynamoDB Accelerator cluster in each Region connected to a single DynamoDB table in us-east-1 to cache reads locally while sending all writes to the primary table in the North American Region

D. Use DynamoDB Streams to capture changes in the primary table in us-east-1 and trigger Lambda functions that replicate the changes to DynamoDB tables in the other two Regions for near-real-time synchronization
explanation: Deploy DynamoDB global tables with replicas in us-east-1 is correct because dynamoDB global tables provide fully managed active-active replication across multiple Regions, allowing both reads and writes with single-digit millisecond latency from any Region. Create separate DynamoDB tables in each Region is incorrect because synchronizing every five minutes introduces data staleness and does not provide real-time consistency across Regions. Place a DynamoDB Accelerator cluster in each Region con is incorrect because DAX can only cache reads and all writes must still travel to us-east-1, resulting in high write latency for European and Asian customers.

question: A company stores IoT sensor data in DynamoDB with a partition key of sensor_id and sort key of timestamp. Queries to retrieve the latest readings for a specific sensor are fast, but queries to find all sensors with readings above a threshold value are slow because they require a full table scan. Which solution improves query performance for the threshold-based queries?
options: A. Increase the provisioned read capacity units on the table to allow the full table scan to complete faster by processing more items per second during the threshold-based query execution across all partitions

B. Create a global secondary index with the reading value as the partition key and timestamp as the sort key, allowing direct queries for readings above any threshold without scanning the entire base table

C. Change the base table partition key to the reading value instead of sensor_id so that threshold-based queries can efficiently read only the partitions containing values above the specified threshold directly
D. Export the DynamoDB table to S3 using the DynamoDB export feature and use Amazon Athena to run SQL queries against the exported data, which is more efficient for analytical threshold-based queries
explanation: Create a global secondary index is correct because a global secondary index with reading value as the partition key allows efficient queries for readings above a threshold using a key condition expression, eliminating the need for a full table scan. Increase the provisioned read capacity units on the tab is incorrect because increasing capacity makes the scan faster but still reads every item in the table, which is inefficient and expensive at scale.

question: A company uses DynamoDB to store user session data for a web application. The sessions have a time-to-live of 24 hours after which they should be automatically deleted. The company wants to avoid the operational overhead of running a cleanup job. Which DynamoDB feature handles automatic session expiration?
options: A. Create a DynamoDB Stream that triggers a Lambda function every time an item is written, and the function checks whether the item has expired and deletes it immediately if the creation time is over 24 hours ago

B. Enable DynamoDB Time to Live on the table and add a TTL attribute to each item containing the expiration timestamp as a Unix epoch value, so DynamoDB automatically deletes expired items at no extra cost
C. Configure a CloudWatch Events rule that triggers a Lambda function every hour to scan the DynamoDB table for items older than 24 hours and delete them in batches using the BatchWriteItem API call

D. Set the table provisioned read and write capacity to zero after 24 hours using a scheduled action, which prevents any access to expired data and effectively expires all items by making the table inaccessible
explanation: Enable DynamoDB Time to Live on the table is correct because dynamoDB TTL automatically deletes items after their specified expiration time without additional cost or operational overhead, requiring only a TTL attribute on each item. Create a DynamoDB Stream is incorrect because dynamoDB Streams trigger on item changes, not on time-based conditions, so a Lambda function would not be invoked when an item expires naturally. Configure a CloudWatch Events rule is incorrect because running a scheduled Lambda to scan and delete items adds operational overhead and cost, which the company wants to avoid.

question: An online auction platform stores bid data in DynamoDB. During the final seconds of popular auctions, thousands of users submit bids simultaneously targeting the same item. The item partition experiences severe throttling due to the concentrated write traffic on a single partition key. Which design change reduces throttling?
options: A. Switch the DynamoDB table to on-demand capacity mode which eliminates throttling by automatically scaling to handle any write volume, including concentrated traffic on a single hot partition key item
B. Use an SQS queue to buffer incoming bids and process them sequentially with a consumer that writes to DynamoDB at a controlled rate, smoothing out the burst of writes over a longer time period

C. Implement a write-behind caching layer using ElastiCache Redis that absorbs the burst of bids and asynchronously writes them to DynamoDB after the auction ends, reducing the concurrent write pressure
D. Append a random suffix to the partition key to distribute writes across multiple partitions, and aggregate results from all partitions when determining the winning bid after the auction concludes
explanation: Use an SQS queue to buffer incoming bids is correct because an SQS queue absorbs the burst of concurrent writes and a consumer processes them at a rate that DynamoDB can handle, preventing throttling on the hot partition while ensuring all bids are eventually recorded. Switch the DynamoDB table to on-demand capacity mode is incorrect because on-demand mode scales table-level capacity but does not eliminate per-partition throttling limits, which remain at 1000 write capacity units per second per partition.

question: A company uses DynamoDB for a product inventory system. The application must decrement the inventory count when an order is placed while ensuring that the count never goes below zero, even under high concurrency. Which DynamoDB feature guarantees this atomic conditional update?
options: A. Use DynamoDB Streams to capture the inventory change event and trigger a Lambda function that validates the count is not negative, rolling back the change by incrementing the count if it went below zero

B. Read the current inventory count using a strongly consistent read, check the count in the application, and if it is above zero, issue an UpdateItem to decrement it, retrying if a ConditionalCheckFailedException occurs

C. Use a DynamoDB transaction with a TransactWriteItems operation that includes a condition expression checking that the current inventory count is greater than zero before applying the decrement to the item

D. Implement optimistic locking by adding a version attribute to each item and including a condition on the version in the UpdateItem request so the update only succeeds if no other process has modified the item since it was read
explanation: Use a DynamoDB transaction is correct because dynamoDB transactions provide atomic conditional writes where the condition expression is evaluated atomically with the update, guaranteeing that the inventory count is checked and decremented in a single atomic operation that prevents the count from going below zero. Use DynamoDB Streams to capture the inventory change event is incorrect because dynamoDB Streams processing occurs after the write, meaning the inventory count could temporarily be negative before the Lambda function corrects it, creating a race condition.

question: A web application uses Amazon ElastiCache for Redis as a session store. When a Redis node fails, users are logged out because their session data is lost. The company needs session data to survive node failures without requiring users to re-authenticate. Which configuration provides this resilience?
options: A. Enable Redis cluster mode with multiple shards and replicas in different AZs, with automatic failover enabled so that if a primary node fails, a replica is promoted and session data is preserved from replication

B. Increase the Redis node instance type to a memory-optimized instance with more RAM so the node can handle more sessions and is less likely to experience failures due to memory exhaustion during peak loads

C. Configure the application to store sessions in both Redis and a DynamoDB table simultaneously, and read from DynamoDB when Redis is unavailable, accepting slightly higher latency during Redis failures

D. Enable Redis append-only file persistence and schedule hourly snapshots to S3 so that session data can be recovered by launching a new Redis node and loading the most recent snapshot after a node failure
explanation: Enable Redis cluster mode with multiple shards is correct because redis cluster mode with replicas in different AZs and automatic failover ensures that when a primary node fails, a replica with a synchronized copy of all session data is automatically promoted, preserving sessions without user impact. Increase the Redis node instance type to a memory-optim is incorrect because a larger instance type does not protect against node failures and only delays memory-related issues.

question: A news website uses ElastiCache for Redis to cache database query results. The cache hit ratio is only 40 percent because popular articles expire from cache while less popular content occupies cache memory. The company wants to improve the cache hit ratio for frequently accessed content. Which strategy addresses this problem?
options: A. Implement a tiered TTL strategy where popular articles get longer cache TTLs and less frequently accessed content gets shorter TTLs, keeping popular content in cache while expiring cold content faster

B. Increase the Redis node instance type to provide more memory so all content, both popular and unpopular, can remain in cache longer without being evicted, naturally improving the overall cache hit ratio
C. Switch from ElastiCache for Redis to ElastiCache for Memcached which uses a more efficient memory allocation algorithm and can store more items in the same amount of available memory capacity

D. Disable cache expiration entirely by setting TTL to zero on all cached items so that content never expires from cache, ensuring the maximum possible hit ratio for all content regardless of popularity level
explanation: Implement a tiered TTL strategy where popular articles is correct because a tiered TTL strategy ensures frequently accessed content stays in cache longer while infrequently accessed content expires sooner, freeing memory for popular items and improving the overall cache hit ratio. Increase the Redis node instance type to provide more m is incorrect because simply adding more memory without changing the TTL strategy will still evict popular content when the larger cache fills up, and is a more expensive approach.

question: A company uses ElastiCache for Redis to cache product pricing data. The application first checks Redis and falls back to the database on a cache miss. During a cache refresh, the database receives a burst of queries because many cached items expire at the same time. This cache stampede overwhelms the database. Which technique prevents this problem?
options: A. Increase the database instance size to handle the burst of queries during cache refresh events, provisioning enough capacity to serve all requests directly when the cache is being repopulated after expiration

B. Add random jitter to each cache entry TTL so that items expire at different times rather than simultaneously, spreading the database load from cache misses over a longer period and preventing the stampede

C. Disable TTL on all cached items and implement manual cache invalidation triggered by a scheduled Lambda function that refreshes one item at a time sequentially from the database during off-peak hours only

D. Configure the ElastiCache cluster with read replicas and distribute the cache miss queries across replicas so that no single Redis node becomes a bottleneck during the cache refresh period affecting the database
explanation: Add random jitter to each cache entry TTL so is correct because adding random jitter to TTLs prevents synchronized expiration, ensuring cache misses are distributed over time rather than occurring simultaneously, which prevents the database from being overwhelmed. Increase the database instance size to handle the burst is incorrect because provisioning the database for peak cache miss load is wasteful during normal operations when the cache is serving most requests.

question: A real-time analytics dashboard queries ElastiCache for Redis to display metrics that are updated every second. The Redis cluster runs in a single AZ.
