The operations team needs the cache to survive an AZ failure without data loss. Which configuration provides the required cross-AZ resilience?
options: A. Create a second independent Redis cluster in another AZ and implement dual-write logic in the application to write analytics data to both clusters simultaneously, reading from whichever cluster is available

B. Enable Multi-AZ with automatic failover on the Redis replication group so that a replica in a different AZ is automatically promoted to primary if the current AZ experiences an outage, preserving cached data

C. Enable Redis RDB snapshots every minute and store them in S3 with cross-Region replication so that a new cluster can be created from the latest snapshot in another AZ if the primary AZ becomes unavailable

D. Switch from ElastiCache for Redis to ElastiCache for Memcached with nodes distributed across multiple AZs, since Memcached natively distributes data across nodes providing built-in AZ redundancy for the dataset
explanation: Enable Multi-AZ with automatic failover on the Redis re is correct because multi-AZ with automatic failover maintains a synchronous replica in another AZ that is promoted automatically during an AZ failure, preserving the cached data with minimal downtime. Create a second independent Redis cluster in another AZ is incorrect because dual-write logic adds application complexity and may result in data inconsistency if one write succeeds and the other fails.

question: A company runs a leaderboard feature for its mobile game using ElastiCache for Redis sorted sets. The leaderboard must support millions of players with real-time score updates and ranked retrieval. The current single-node Redis setup is approaching memory limits. Which scaling approach accommodates growth while maintaining sorted set functionality?
options: A. Enable Redis cluster mode with data sharding across multiple nodes so that the sorted set data is distributed across shards, allowing horizontal scaling of memory capacity beyond a single node limit
B. Add read replicas to the existing Redis replication group to distribute read traffic across more nodes, which reduces the memory pressure on the primary node by offloading sorted set range queries

C. Migrate from Redis to DynamoDB with a global secondary index on the score attribute, which provides unlimited storage capacity and supports efficient range queries for leaderboard retrieval at any scale

D. Vertically scale the Redis node to the largest available instance type with the maximum memory capacity, which provides enough memory for millions of players without requiring any application changes
explanation: Enable Redis cluster mode is correct because redis cluster mode distributes data across multiple shards, each on its own node, allowing the total memory capacity to scale horizontally well beyond any single node limit while maintaining sorted set operations within each shard. Add read replicas to the existing Redis replication gro is incorrect because read replicas contain the same data as the primary and do not increase total memory capacity, which is the bottleneck in this scenario.

question: A company uses ElastiCache for Redis to cache API responses. The development team wants to implement cache invalidation that updates the cache whenever the underlying database record changes. The database is Amazon Aurora MySQL. Which approach provides reliable cache invalidation with minimal latency?
options: A. Implement a cache-aside pattern where the application invalidates the Redis cache entry immediately after writing to Aurora, ensuring the next read fetches fresh data from the database and repopulates the cache

B. Configure Aurora event notifications to send messages to an SNS topic whenever a database modification occurs, and subscribe a Lambda function that invalidates the corresponding Redis cache entry immediately

C. Set very short TTLs on all Redis cache entries so that stale data is never served for more than a few seconds, relying on natural expiration rather than explicit invalidation after database changes occur

D. Create a DynamoDB table that tracks all database changes and run a scheduled Lambda function every minute that reads the change log and invalidates the corresponding Redis cache entries in a batch operation
explanation: Implement a cache-aside pattern where the application i is correct because the cache-aside pattern with write-through invalidation provides reliable cache invalidation by having the application code explicitly delete or update the cache entry immediately after a database write, ensuring subsequent reads get fresh data. Configure Aurora event notifications to send messages t is incorrect because aurora does not provide real-time event notifications for individual row-level database modifications, only for operational events like failovers and maintenance.

question: A content management system runs on multiple EC2 instances behind an ALB. Each instance needs access to the same set of uploaded media files. Currently, files uploaded through one instance are not visible to other instances. Which storage solution provides shared access across all instances?
options: A. Create a large EBS volume and use Multi-Attach to connect it to all EC2 instances simultaneously so that each instance can read and write to the shared volume for uploaded media files

B. Create an Amazon EFS file system and mount it on all EC2 instances at the same mount point so that files uploaded through any instance are immediately visible to all other instances sharing the mount
C. Configure each EC2 instance to sync its local storage to an S3 bucket every five minutes using the AWS CLI and have other instances download new files from the bucket on the same schedule
D. Create an EBS volume for each instance and use AWS DataSync to replicate data between all EBS volumes every minute so that changes on one volume are propagated to all other instance volumes
explanation: Create an Amazon EFS file system is correct because amazon EFS is a fully managed NFS file system that supports concurrent access from multiple EC2 instances across AZs, providing immediate visibility of uploaded files to all instances. Create a large EBS volume is incorrect because EBS Multi-Attach is limited to io1/io2 volumes within a single AZ and requires a cluster-aware file system, making it unsuitable for general-purpose shared file storage.

question: A company uses Amazon EFS for shared storage across its application fleet. The file system stores a mix of frequently accessed configuration files and rarely accessed log archives. Storage costs are growing rapidly. Which EFS feature reduces costs without impacting access to frequently used files?
options: A. Create two separate EFS file systems, one for configuration files using Standard storage and one for log archives using a smaller throughput mode, and mount them at different paths on each instance

B. Compress all files stored on the EFS file system using gzip to reduce the amount of storage consumed, requiring the application to decompress files before reading and compress before writing each time

C. Switch from EFS to S3 for all file storage since S3 offers lower per-GB pricing than EFS, and modify the application to use the S3 API instead of file system operations for reading and writing files
D. Enable EFS lifecycle management to automatically transition files not accessed within a defined period to the EFS Infrequent Access storage class, reducing costs for rarely accessed log archives
explanation: Enable EFS lifecycle management to automatically transi is correct because EFS lifecycle management automatically moves files that have not been accessed within the configured period to the Infrequent Access storage class, which costs up to 92 percent less than Standard, without requiring any application changes. Create two separate EFS file systems is incorrect because managing two separate file systems adds operational complexity and requires application changes to use different mount points.

question: A machine learning team processes large datasets stored on Amazon EFS. Training jobs run on a cluster of EC2 instances that read the same dataset files concurrently. The team reports that read throughput drops significantly when multiple instances access the file system simultaneously. Which EFS configuration change improves concurrent read performance?
options: A. Switch from General Purpose to Max I/O performance mode on the EFS file system, which is optimized for highly parallelized workloads that need to support thousands of concurrent clients accessing the data

B. Enable EFS Intelligent-Tiering to automatically place frequently accessed data on faster storage media while moving cold data to slower tiers, improving read performance for the active training dataset

C. Increase the EFS provisioned throughput to a higher value in provisioned throughput mode, ensuring the file system can deliver the aggregate bandwidth needed by all concurrent EC2 instances reading simultaneously

D. Create multiple copies of the dataset on the same EFS file system in different directories and configure each EC2 instance to read from a different directory to reduce contention on the same set of files
explanation: Increase the EFS provisioned throughput to a higher val is correct because provisioned throughput mode allows setting a throughput level independent of the amount of data stored, ensuring sufficient bandwidth for all concurrent readers. If the file system is in bursting mode, throughput may be insufficient for sustained parallel reads. Switch from General Purpose to Max I/O performance mode is incorrect because max I/O mode increases the number of concurrent operations but can add latency per operation, and General Purpose mode is typically sufficient for most workloads since it was enhanced to support higher throughput levels.

question: A company runs a serverless application that uses AWS Lambda functions to process files. The functions need access to a shared file system that contains reference data updated by an external process. Amazon S3 is not suitable because the processing code uses standard file system read operations. Which storage solution allows Lambda to access shared file data?
options: A. Attach an EBS volume to the Lambda execution environment by configuring the function with a block device mapping that references the volume ID, allowing standard file read operations on the mounted volume

B. Use the Lambda ephemeral storage feature to provide up to 10 GB of temporary local storage and copy the reference data from S3 to the tmp directory at the start of each function invocation for processing

C. Mount an Amazon EFS file system to the Lambda functions by configuring the function to run within a VPC and specifying the EFS access point, enabling standard file system reads from the shared data store

D. Create an Amazon FSx for Lustre file system linked to an S3 bucket and configure the Lambda function to mount the Lustre file system for high-performance parallel file reads during function execution
explanation: Mount an Amazon EFS file system to the Lambda functions is correct because lambda natively supports mounting EFS file systems via EFS access points when the function is configured to run in a VPC, enabling standard file system operations on shared data. Attach an EBS volume to the Lambda execution environmen is incorrect because lambda functions cannot attach EBS volumes since EBS requires an EC2 instance host for block device mounting.

question: A company operates a web application that stores user uploads on Amazon EFS. The security team requires that all data stored on the file system be encrypted and that access be restricted to specific application processes running under designated Linux user IDs. Which configuration meets both requirements?
options: A. Enable EFS encryption in transit only by configuring the EFS mount helper with TLS and use Linux file permissions on the EFS mount point to restrict access to specific user IDs without access points

B. Create an IAM policy that restricts efs:ClientMount to specific IAM roles and enable EFS encryption at rest, relying on IAM authentication alone to control which Linux processes can read and write files

C. Deploy the EFS file system in a private subnet with no internet gateway and configure security groups to allow traffic only from the application instances, using network isolation as the access control mechanism

D. Enable EFS encryption at rest using an AWS KMS key and create EFS access points with POSIX user and group enforcement to restrict file access to specific Linux user IDs defined by the application processes
explanation: Enable EFS encryption at rest using an AWS KMS key is correct because EFS encryption at rest with a KMS key encrypts all stored data, and EFS access points enforce POSIX user and group IDs on all file operations, ensuring only designated application processes can access the files. Enable EFS encryption in transit only by configuring th is incorrect because encryption in transit does not satisfy the at-rest encryption requirement, and standard Linux file permissions on EFS can be overridden by root users without access point enforcement.

question: A company runs its microservices application on Amazon ECS with Fargate launch type. During traffic spikes, some tasks take 3 minutes to start because container images are large. This startup delay causes request timeouts for users. Which approach reduces task startup time?
options: A. Switch from Fargate to EC2 launch type with pre-pulled container images cached on the instances so that ECS tasks start without waiting for image download during scaling events
B. Increase the Fargate task CPU and memory allocation to the maximum available values so that the container image downloads and decompresses faster during task provisioning and startup
C. Configure Amazon CloudFront to cache container images at edge locations closer to the Fargate infrastructure so that image pulls complete faster due to reduced network latency from the registry

D. Optimize container images by using smaller base images like Alpine Linux, implementing multi-stage Docker builds, and removing unnecessary files to reduce the overall image size downloaded at startup
explanation: Optimize container images by is correct because smaller container images download and decompress faster, directly reducing the time between task scheduling and the container becoming ready to serve traffic. Multi-stage builds and minimal base images are best practices for this optimization. Switch from Fargate to EC2 launch type is incorrect because switching to EC2 launch type requires managing instance scaling and capacity, adding operational overhead that conflicts with the benefits of Fargate. Increase the Fargate task CPU is incorrect because fargate task CPU and memory settings affect the running container, not the speed of image download from the container registry.

question: A company deploys a web application on ECS Fargate with tasks running in a single AZ. During a recent AZ outage, all tasks became unavailable. The architect must redesign the deployment to survive AZ failures. Which configuration provides AZ-level resilience for Fargate tasks?
options: A. Create separate ECS services in each AZ with one task each and use Route 53 weighted routing to distribute traffic between the services, with health checks to remove unhealthy AZ services from DNS

B. Configure the ECS service to use subnets across multiple AZs in the network configuration and set the desired count to at least two tasks so that tasks are distributed across AZs automatically by ECS
C. Deploy the ECS tasks on Fargate Spot capacity across multiple AZs to reduce costs while ensuring that if one AZ fails, Spot capacity in other AZs continues to run tasks and serve traffic
D. Configure an Auto Scaling group of EC2 instances across multiple AZs and switch from Fargate to EC2 launch type so that ECS tasks benefit from the AZ distribution of the underlying EC2 instances
explanation: Configure the ECS service to use subnets across multipl is correct because configuring the ECS service with subnets in multiple AZs causes Fargate to distribute tasks across those AZs automatically, and having at least two tasks ensures that losing one AZ still leaves tasks running in other AZs. Create separate ECS services in each AZ with one task each is incorrect because managing separate services per AZ adds unnecessary complexity when ECS natively supports multi-AZ task distribution within a single service.

question: A company runs a batch processing application on ECS with Fargate. The application needs to process files from S3 and write results back to S3. Each task processes one file and takes between 5 and 30 minutes. The company wants to automatically scale the number of tasks based on the number of unprocessed files. Which scaling mechanism is appropriate?
options: A. Configure ECS Service Auto Scaling with a target tracking policy based on CPU utilization, setting the target to 70 percent so that more tasks are launched when the existing tasks are heavily loaded
B. Use an SQS queue where each message represents a file to process, and configure ECS Service Auto Scaling with a step scaling policy triggered by CloudWatch alarms on the SQS queue depth metric

C. Create a scheduled ECS task that runs every hour and launches enough tasks to process all remaining files in the S3 bucket, using the S3 inventory report to determine the number of unprocessed files

D. Configure an S3 event notification that triggers a Lambda function for each new file, and the Lambda function calls the ECS RunTask API to start a new Fargate task for each individual file uploaded
explanation: Use an SQS queue where each message represents a file t is correct because using SQS to queue files for processing and scaling ECS tasks based on queue depth ensures the number of processing tasks matches the backlog of unprocessed files. Step scaling adds tasks proportionally as the queue grows. Configure ECS Service Auto Scaling is incorrect because CPU utilization does not reflect the number of unprocessed files and may not trigger scaling when tasks are waiting for I/O rather than consuming CPU.

question: A company runs containers on ECS Fargate and needs to update its application to a new version without downtime. The update must gradually shift traffic to the new version and automatically roll back if the new version health checks fail. Which deployment strategy meets these requirements?
options: A. Use ECS rolling update deployment with a maximum percent of 200 and minimum healthy percent of 100, which replaces tasks one at a time and stops if new tasks fail to reach a healthy running state

B. Use AWS CodeDeploy with a blue/green deployment for ECS that shifts traffic gradually using canary or linear traffic shifting and automatically rolls back if CloudWatch alarms indicate health check failures

C. Manually create a second ECS service running the new version and use ALB weighted target groups to gradually shift traffic, then delete the old service after verifying the new version works correctly

D. Stop all running tasks in the ECS service, update the task definition to the new version, and start new tasks, accepting a brief downtime window while the old tasks are stopped and new tasks are launched
explanation: Use AWS CodeDeploy is correct because codeDeploy blue/green deployment for ECS supports canary and linear traffic shifting strategies that gradually route traffic to the new version, with automatic rollback triggered by CloudWatch alarm failures. Use ECS rolling update deployment is incorrect because rolling updates replace tasks but do not support gradual traffic shifting or automatic rollback based on custom health metrics. Manually create a second ECS service running the new ve is incorrect because manual traffic shifting does not provide automatic rollback and requires significant operational effort.

question: A company has multiple ECS services that need to communicate with each other. Service A calls Service B via its ALB endpoint. When Service B scales down, Service A occasionally receives connection errors because DNS resolves to IPs of recently terminated tasks. Which approach provides more reliable service-to-service communication?
options: A. Increase the DNS TTL on the ALB to 300 seconds so that Service A caches the ALB IP addresses longer and does not receive stale individual task IP addresses that may belong to terminated tasks

B. Use AWS Cloud Map for service discovery with ECS service connect, which provides automatic registration and deregistration of tasks and routes traffic directly between services without relying on external DNS caching

C. Configure Service A to retry failed requests to Service B with exponential backoff, accepting occasional connection errors as normal during scaling events and relying on retries to eventually reach healthy tasks

D. Deploy a Network Load Balancer instead of an ALB for Service B because NLBs use static IP addresses that never change during scaling events, eliminating DNS resolution issues for service-to-service calls
explanation: Use AWS Cloud Map for service discovery is correct because AWS Cloud Map with ECS service connect provides real-time service discovery where task registrations and deregistrations are reflected immediately, avoiding stale DNS entries and providing reliable routing between services. Increase the DNS TTL on the ALB to 300 seconds so is incorrect because increasing DNS TTL would make the stale DNS problem worse, not better, as cached IPs would point to terminated tasks for even longer.

question: A company runs a critical API service on ECS Fargate. The service must maintain at least four healthy tasks at all times, even during deployments. The current configuration has a desired count of four, but during rolling updates the healthy task count temporarily drops to three. Which setting ensures four healthy tasks are maintained during updates?
options: A. Set the minimum healthy percent to 100 and the maximum percent to 200, which ensures the old tasks remain running until new tasks are healthy before any old tasks are stopped during the rolling update

B. Set the minimum healthy percent to 50 and the maximum percent to 100, which allows ECS to stop two tasks at a time and launch their replacements while maintaining at least two tasks running at all times
C. Increase the desired count to 8 tasks permanently so that even if the rolling update stops half the tasks at any time, there are still four healthy tasks available to handle incoming API requests
D. Set the minimum healthy percent to 100 and the maximum percent to 100, which forces ECS to maintain exactly four tasks at all times and replace each old task with a new one simultaneously in place
explanation: Set the minimum healthy percent to 100 is correct because setting minimum healthy percent to 100 ensures all four existing tasks remain running during the update, and maximum percent of 200 allows ECS to launch up to four additional new tasks. Old tasks are only stopped after new tasks pass health checks. Set the minimum healthy percent to 50 is incorrect because setting both to 100 creates a deadlock where ECS cannot launch new tasks because the maximum is reached and cannot stop old tasks because the minimum must be maintained.

question: A company runs its application on Amazon EKS with worker nodes in a single Availability Zone. During a recent AZ outage, the entire application went offline. The architect must ensure the EKS cluster survives AZ failures. Which configuration provides this resilience?
options: A. Create a second EKS cluster in a different AZ as a standby and use Route 53 failover routing to switch traffic from the primary cluster to the standby cluster automatically when the primary AZ fails

B. Increase the number of worker nodes in the single AZ to provide redundancy, and configure the Kubernetes cluster autoscaler to launch replacement nodes quickly if some nodes fail during an AZ outage

C. Switch from EKS managed node groups to self-managed EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group within the same AZ, which provides faster instance replacement than managed nodes during AZ infrastructure issues

D. Deploy EKS managed node groups across three AZs and configure pod anti-affinity rules to spread replicas across different AZs so that an AZ failure does not take down all replicas of any application component
explanation: Deploy EKS managed node groups across three AZs is correct because deploying managed node groups across multiple AZs ensures worker nodes exist in multiple AZs, and pod anti-affinity rules distribute application replicas across AZs so that losing one AZ does not affect all replicas. Create a second EKS cluster in a different AZ as a standby is incorrect because running a second EKS cluster as a standby adds significant cost and operational complexity when multi-AZ node groups within a single cluster provide AZ resilience natively.

question: A company runs a stateful application on EKS that stores data on Amazon EBS volumes attached to pods. When a pod is rescheduled to a node in a different AZ, the EBS volume cannot follow because EBS volumes are AZ-specific. Which approach allows stateful pods to persist data across AZ failures?
options: A. Configure the Kubernetes persistent volume claim to use the gp3 volume type with Multi-Attach enabled so the same EBS volume can be attached to nodes in different AZs simultaneously without data loss
B. Pre-create EBS volumes in every AZ and configure the EBS CSI driver to snapshot the volume and create a new volume from the snapshot in the target AZ before the pod starts on the new node location
C. Switch from EBS volumes to Amazon EFS for pod storage using the EFS CSI driver, which provides a file system accessible from any AZ allowing pods to be rescheduled to any node without losing data
D. Use Kubernetes node affinity rules to pin stateful pods to a specific AZ permanently, preventing rescheduling to other AZs and avoiding the cross-AZ EBS volume limitation by keeping pods in one AZ
explanation: Switch from EBS volumes to Amazon EFS for pod storage is correct because amazon EFS is accessible from any AZ within a Region, so pods rescheduled to nodes in different AZs can continue accessing the same data without volume migration. The EFS CSI driver integrates natively with Kubernetes persistent volume claims. Configure the Kubernetes persistent volume claim to use is incorrect because EBS Multi-Attach works only within a single AZ and only with io1/io2 volumes, so it cannot attach across AZs.

question: A company uses Amazon EKS with the Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler to scale application pods based on CPU utilization. During sudden traffic bursts, pod scaling is blocked because there are no available nodes with sufficient resources to schedule new pods. Which component resolves this node capacity bottleneck?
options: A. Replace the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler with a Vertical Pod Autoscaler that increases the CPU and memory allocation of existing pods instead of creating new pods, eliminating the need for additional nodes
B. Configure the EKS managed node group with a fixed number of very large instances so there is always enough spare capacity on existing nodes to schedule new pods without needing additional nodes
C. Switch from EKS managed node groups to Fargate profiles so that each pod runs on its own dedicated compute without requiring shared node capacity management or cluster-level autoscaling logic
D. Deploy the Kubernetes Cluster Autoscaler or Karpenter which detects pending pods that cannot be scheduled due to insufficient resources and automatically provisions new nodes to accommodate them
explanation: Deploy the Kubernetes Cluster Autoscaler or Karpenter is correct because the Cluster Autoscaler and Karpenter monitor for pods in a pending state due to insufficient node resources and automatically add nodes to the cluster, resolving the capacity bottleneck during scaling events. Replace the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler is incorrect because the Vertical Pod Autoscaler increases resources for existing pods but does not add more pod replicas to handle increased traffic, and resizing requires pod restarts.

question: A company runs EKS clusters in three Regions for a globally distributed application. The team needs to deploy the same Kubernetes manifests and configurations consistently across all clusters while detecting and correcting any configuration drift. Which approach achieves this?
options: A. Write a shell script that applies kubectl commands to all three clusters sequentially from a bastion host and schedule the script to run every hour using a cron job to detect and fix configuration drift

B. Store Kubernetes manifests in a Git repository and use a GitOps tool like Flux or ArgoCD deployed on each cluster to continuously reconcile the cluster state with the desired state defined in the repository

C. Use AWS CloudFormation StackSets to deploy Kubernetes resources across all three Regions simultaneously, leveraging CloudFormation drift detection to identify and correct any configuration differences

D. Create separate CI/CD pipelines for each Region using AWS CodePipeline that independently build and deploy from the same source repository, relying on identical pipeline configurations to maintain consistency
explanation: Store Kubernetes manifests in a Git repository is correct because gitOps tools like Flux and ArgoCD continuously monitor a Git repository for desired state and automatically reconcile any drift on each cluster, ensuring consistent configuration across all Regions. Write a shell script is incorrect because a shell script with kubectl is fragile, does not handle partial failures gracefully, and hourly execution leaves gaps where drift goes undetected. Use AWS CloudFormation StackSets to deploy Kubernetes r is incorrect because cloudFormation does not natively manage Kubernetes resources and StackSets would need custom resources, adding significant complexity.

question: A company runs an EKS cluster where different teams deploy workloads with varying resource requirements. One team deployed a workload that consumed all available cluster memory, causing other teams' pods to be evicted. Which Kubernetes feature prevents a single team from consuming excessive cluster resources?
options: A. Deploy a Kubernetes PodSecurityPolicy that restricts the maximum memory and CPU each container can request, preventing any individual pod from requesting excessive resources regardless of the namespace

B. Configure the Kubernetes scheduler with a custom scoring plugin that prioritizes pods from teams with lower resource usage and delays scheduling for teams that have already consumed their fair share

C. Set up Kubernetes pod priority classes for each team and configure preemption rules so that pods from teams that have exceeded their fair share are automatically evicted when cluster resources are constrained

D. Configure Kubernetes ResourceQuota objects on each team namespace to set limits on the total CPU and memory that all pods in the namespace can consume, preventing any team from exceeding its allocation
explanation: Configure Kubernetes ResourceQuota objects on each team is correct because resourceQuota objects enforce hard limits on the total amount of CPU, memory, and other resources that can be consumed by all pods within a namespace, effectively preventing any single team from monopolizing cluster resources. Deploy a Kubernetes PodSecurityPolicy is incorrect because podSecurityPolicy is deprecated in newer Kubernetes versions and was focused on security constraints, not resource limits, and LimitRange is the correct object for per-pod defaults but does not cap total namespace usage.

question: A company processes insurance claims through a multi-step workflow that includes validation, fraud detection, approval, and payment. Each step calls a different Lambda function. If the fraud detection step fails, the claim should be flagged for manual review instead of failing the entire workflow. Which AWS service coordinates this workflow with error handling?
options: A. Use Amazon SQS with separate queues for each step, implementing the state machine logic in the consumer Lambda functions that read from one queue and write to the next, with error queues for failed steps

B. Use Amazon EventBridge with rules that chain Lambda functions in sequence, configuring a dead-letter queue on the fraud detection rule to capture failed events and notify the manual review team via email

C. Use AWS Step Functions with a state machine that defines each step as a Task state, using a Catch block on the fraud detection step that transitions to a manual review state instead of failing the execution

D. Use AWS Lambda destinations configured on each function to route successful results to the next function and failed results to an SNS topic that notifies the review team for any step that encounters an error
explanation: Use AWS Step Functions is correct because step Functions provides a visual workflow orchestration service where each step is a Task state, and Catch blocks on individual states allow specific error handling such as routing fraud detection failures to a manual review state without terminating the workflow. Use Amazon SQS with separate queues for each step is incorrect because implementing state machine logic across multiple SQS queues and Lambda consumers creates significant complexity and is difficult to maintain and monitor compared to Step Functions.

question: A company runs a data pipeline that processes files through five sequential Lambda functions. Occasionally, the third function fails due to a transient database connectivity issue. The team currently restarts the entire pipeline manually when this happens. Which Step Functions feature enables the pipeline to resume from the failed step without reprocessing earlier steps?
options: A. Configure a Retry policy on the third task state with exponential backoff and a maximum of three attempts so that transient failures are automatically retried before the execution is considered failed

B. Configure the state machine with RedrivePolicy and use the redrive feature to restart a failed execution from the failed state rather than from the beginning, preserving the results of earlier successful steps

C. Enable Step Functions Express Workflows which are optimized for high-throughput short-duration workflows and include built-in retry logic that automatically resumes from the last successful step on failure

D. Store the output of each step in a DynamoDB table and add a check at the beginning of each function that skips processing if the output already exists, effectively creating checkpoints throughout the pipeline
explanation: Configure the state machine with RedrivePolicy is correct because step Functions redrive allows restarting a failed Standard Workflow execution from the point of failure, preserving the results of previously completed states and avoiding reprocessing of earlier steps. Configure a Retry policy on the third task state is incorrect because while Retry handles transient failures automatically, if all retries are exhausted the execution still fails and must be restarted entirely without the redrive feature.

question: A company needs to process 10,000 customer records simultaneously, where each record requires an independent API call that takes 2 seconds. Running these sequentially would take over 5 hours. The company wants to process all records in parallel to reduce the total execution time. Which Step Functions feature enables this parallel processing?
options: A. Create a Parallel state with 10,000 branches, each containing a Task state that processes one customer record, allowing all records to be processed simultaneously within a single state machine execution

B. Use a Map state in distributed mode to process each of the 10,000 records as a separate child execution in parallel, with configurable concurrency limits to control the rate of API calls to the downstream service

C. Split the 10,000 records into 10 batches of 1,000 and create 10 separate state machine executions using a parent orchestrator state machine, with each child execution processing its batch sequentially

D. Use a Choice state that routes each record to one of 10 Lambda functions based on the record index, with each Lambda function processing 1,000 records internally using multithreading for parallel processing
explanation: Use a Map state in distributed mode to process each of is correct because step Functions Distributed Map mode can process thousands of items in parallel by running each item as a separate child execution, with configurable concurrency to prevent overwhelming downstream services. Create a Parallel state with 10 is incorrect because a Parallel state requires each branch to be defined in the state machine definition at design time and cannot dynamically create 10,000 branches from runtime data.

question: A company uses Step Functions to orchestrate a long-running order fulfillment workflow. After the payment step, the workflow must wait for an external warehouse system to confirm shipment, which can take 1 to 5 days. The warehouse system communicates via webhooks. Which pattern allows the workflow to pause and resume when the webhook arrives?
options: A. Use a Task state with a callback pattern that generates a unique task token, sends it to the warehouse system, and pauses execution until the warehouse calls back with the token via the SendTaskSuccess API

B. Use a Wait state configured with a 5-day timeout, and have the webhook trigger a Lambda function that terminates the Wait state early by sending a signal to the Step Functions execution using the SendTaskSuccess API

C. Use a Lambda function that polls the warehouse API every hour to check for shipment confirmation, with the Step Functions execution continuing to the next step only after the Lambda returns a successful result

D. End the Step Functions execution after the payment step and create a new execution when the webhook arrives, passing the order details from the webhook payload to restart the workflow from the shipment step
explanation: Use a Task state is correct because the callback pattern with task tokens allows Step Functions to pause execution indefinitely until an external system calls SendTaskSuccess or SendTaskFailure with the token, which is the standard pattern for integrating with external asynchronous systems. Use a Wait state configured with a 5-day timeout is incorrect because wait states pause for a fixed duration or until a specific time and cannot be interrupted by external signals.

question: A company uses Step Functions to coordinate a nightly ETL pipeline that extracts data from three independent sources, transforms each dataset, and loads the combined results into a data warehouse. The three extractions can run simultaneously but all must complete before the transform step begins. Which state machine design implements this correctly?
options: A. Use three sequential Task states for the extractions followed by the transform Task state, with each extraction passing its output to the next extraction state as input for the subsequent processing step

B. Use a Map state that iterates over an array of three data source configurations and runs the extraction for each source, followed by the transform Task state after the Map state completes all iterations

C. Use a Parallel state containing three branches each with an extraction Task state, followed by a transform Task state that receives the combined output array from all three branches after they all complete

D. Use three separate state machine executions triggered by an EventBridge rule, each processing one extraction, and a fourth execution that starts after CloudWatch detects all three executions have succeeded
explanation: Use a Parallel state containing three branches each is correct because the Parallel state runs all branches concurrently and waits for all to complete before transitioning to the next state, and the combined output from all branches is passed as an array to the transform Task state. Use three sequential Task states for the extractions fo is incorrect because while a Map state could process the three sources, it is designed for iterating over items with the same processing logic, whereas Parallel is specifically designed for running different concurrent branches.

question: A company needs to trigger different actions when an EC2 instance changes state. When an instance enters the running state, a configuration management tool should be invoked. When an instance is terminated, a cleanup Lambda function should remove associated resources. Which service coordinates these event-driven responses?
options: A. Create a CloudWatch alarm for each instance that monitors the instance state metric and configure different alarm actions for each state transition, invoking the appropriate target for running and terminated states

B. Enable AWS CloudTrail and create a CloudTrail trail that logs EC2 API calls, then use a Lambda function to parse the trail logs hourly and invoke the appropriate actions based on the RunInstances and TerminateInstances events

C. Configure EC2 user data scripts that run on instance startup to invoke the configuration tool, and create a lifecycle hook on the Auto Scaling group that triggers the cleanup Lambda before instance termination completes

D. Configure Amazon EventBridge rules that match EC2 instance state change events, with one rule filtering for the running state targeting the configuration tool and another rule filtering for terminated targeting the cleanup Lambda
explanation: Configure Amazon EventBridge rules is correct because eventBridge natively receives EC2 instance state change events and allows rules to filter by specific states, routing each event to the appropriate target such as a Lambda function or Systems Manager automation. Create a CloudWatch alarm for each instance is incorrect because cloudWatch alarms monitor metrics, not instance state changes, and cannot differentiate between running and terminated states using metric-based thresholds. Enable AWS CloudTrail is incorrect because parsing CloudTrail logs hourly introduces significant delay and adds complexity compared to near-real-time EventBridge events.

question: A company operates in two AWS Regions and needs events from its production account in us-east-1 to trigger automation workflows in its operations account in eu-west-1. The events include security findings from GuardDuty and compliance changes from AWS Config. Which EventBridge configuration enables this cross-Region, cross-account event routing?
options: A. Create an SNS topic in us-east-1 that receives events from EventBridge, configure cross-Region SNS subscription to an SQS queue in eu-west-1 in the operations account, and process events from the queue using Lambda

B. Export events from the production account EventBridge to a Kinesis Data Stream in us-east-1, replicate the stream to eu-west-1 using Kinesis cross-Region replication, and consume events from the replicated stream

C. Write a Lambda function in the production account that receives all events and uses the AWS SDK to put events directly on the operations account EventBridge bus in eu-west-1, handling authentication with cross-account IAM roles

D. Configure EventBridge rules in the production account to forward matching events to the default event bus in the operations account in eu-west-1, with the operations account event bus resource policy allowing events from the production account
explanation: Configure EventBridge rules in the production account t is correct because eventBridge natively supports cross-Region and cross-account event routing by configuring rules to forward events to event buses in other accounts and Regions, with resource-based policies controlling access. Create an SNS topic in us-east-1 is incorrect because routing through SNS and SQS adds unnecessary intermediate services when EventBridge supports direct cross-account and cross-Region routing. Export events from the production account EventBridge t is incorrect because kinesis Data Streams does not have a native cross-Region replication feature and this approach adds significant complexity.

question: A company uses EventBridge to trigger Lambda functions when files are uploaded to S3. During a Lambda service disruption, events were lost because EventBridge could not deliver them. The company needs to ensure events are not lost during downstream service failures. Which EventBridge feature preserves events during delivery failures?
options: A. Enable EventBridge event replay by creating an archive of all events on the event bus, allowing the team to replay events from the archive after the Lambda service disruption is resolved and functions are available

B. Configure the EventBridge rule with a retry policy that attempts delivery up to 185 times over 24 hours with exponential backoff, ensuring events are eventually delivered once the Lambda service is restored

C. Configure a dead-letter queue on the EventBridge rule target so that events that cannot be delivered to the Lambda function are sent to an SQS queue for later processing after the Lambda service recovers

D. Create a second EventBridge rule that sends a copy of every event to an SQS queue as a backup target, and configure a consumer that processes events from the queue only when the primary Lambda target is unavailable
explanation: Configure a dead-letter queue on the EventBridge rule t is correct because configuring a dead-letter queue on the EventBridge rule target captures events that fail delivery after retry attempts are exhausted, preserving them in SQS for processing after the downstream service recovers. Enable EventBridge event replay by creating an archive is incorrect because event archives and replay require manual initiation and are designed for debugging and reprocessing scenarios rather than automatic recovery from delivery failures.

question: A company runs a scheduled batch job every day at 2 AM UTC using a cron-based EventBridge rule that triggers a Lambda function. The Lambda function occasionally times out during processing. The team needs the job to automatically retry if it fails, with notifications sent after the final retry attempt fails. Which configuration achieves this?
options: A. Create a Step Functions state machine that wraps the Lambda invocation with a Retry policy of 3 attempts and a Catch block that sends an SNS notification, then trigger the state machine from the EventBridge rule

B. Configure the EventBridge rule target with a retry policy of 3 attempts, and set up a dead-letter queue on the target that triggers an SNS notification via a second Lambda function when messages arrive in the DLQ

C. Configure the Lambda function with a maximum retry count of 3 in its event source mapping and add an SNS destination for the on-failure async invocation configuration to send notifications on the final failure

D. Modify the Lambda function code to implement internal retry logic with a counter, and when the counter reaches three failed attempts, the function publishes a message to an SNS topic before exiting with an error
explanation: Create a Step Functions state machine is correct because step Functions provides robust retry with configurable backoff and error handling through Catch blocks, allowing the workflow to send an SNS notification after all retry attempts are exhausted. This is the most reliable approach for managed retries with error handling. Configure the EventBridge rule target is incorrect because eventBridge retry policies apply to event delivery to the target, not to the target execution outcome, meaning a Lambda timeout would be considered a successful delivery.

question: A company needs to decouple its order service from its inventory, shipping, and billing services. When an order is placed, all three downstream services must be notified. The company wants to add new consumer services in the future without modifying the order service. Which EventBridge pattern achieves this loose coupling?
options: A. Have the order service call each downstream service API directly using the AWS SDK and maintain a configuration file listing all target services, adding new entries to the file when new consumers are added

B. Have the order service publish an OrderPlaced event to a custom EventBridge event bus, and create separate rules for inventory, shipping, and billing that match the event and route to their respective targets

C. Have the order service send messages to three separate SQS queues, one for each downstream service, and create new queues with the order service publishing to them whenever a new consumer service is added

D. Have the order service write order records to a DynamoDB table and configure DynamoDB Streams to trigger a Lambda function that fans out notifications to all downstream services listed in a configuration table
explanation: Have the order service publish an OrderPlaced event to is correct because publishing events to an EventBridge custom event bus allows any number of rules to match the event and route to different targets. New consumer services can be added by creating new rules without modifying the order service. Have the order service call each downstream service API is incorrect because direct API calls create tight coupling between the order service and downstream services, and modifying a configuration file still requires order service changes.

question: A company sends critical system alerts via Amazon SNS to an operations team. During a recent incident, some team members did not receive notifications because their email delivery failed silently. The company needs to ensure alert delivery to all team members. Which approach improves notification reliability?
options: A. Create multiple SNS subscriptions for each team member using different protocols such as email, SMS, and a mobile push notification so that if one channel fails, the member receives the alert on another channel

B. Configure SNS message delivery status logging for the email protocol and set up a CloudWatch alarm that triggers when delivery failures are detected, sending a backup notification through a different channel

C. Increase the SNS message retention period to 7 days so that failed email deliveries are automatically retried over a longer period, increasing the probability that the email will eventually be delivered successfully

D. Switch from SNS email notifications to SES transactional emails triggered by SNS events, since SES provides better email delivery rates and built-in retry logic compared to the standard SNS email protocol
explanation: Create multiple SNS subscriptions for each team member is correct because subscribing each team member through multiple delivery channels provides redundancy, ensuring that if email delivery fails, the alert is still received via SMS or mobile push notification. Configure SNS message delivery status logging for the e is incorrect because detecting email failures and sending backup notifications introduces delay during critical incidents and adds complexity with a secondary notification mechanism.

question: A company publishes events to an SNS topic that has Lambda, SQS, and HTTP endpoint subscriptions. The HTTP endpoint occasionally returns 5xx errors. The company wants failed HTTP deliveries to be preserved for later reprocessing without affecting delivery to the Lambda and SQS subscribers. Which SNS feature enables this?
options: A. Configure a dead-letter queue specifically on the HTTP endpoint subscription so that messages that fail delivery to the HTTP endpoint after retries are sent to an SQS queue for later reprocessing
B. Enable SNS message filtering on the HTTP endpoint subscription to reject messages that would cause the endpoint to return errors, preventing failed deliveries from occurring in the first place

C. Configure the SNS topic delivery retry policy with a maximum of 100 retries over 24 hours for all subscriptions so that the HTTP endpoint has more opportunities to successfully receive the message delivery
D. Create a separate SNS topic for the HTTP endpoint and subscribe it to the original topic using an SQS queue as a buffer, providing the HTTP endpoint with a reliable message store to pull from
explanation: Configure a dead-letter queue specifically on the HTTP is correct because SNS supports subscription-level dead-letter queues, so failed deliveries to the HTTP endpoint are captured in an SQS DLQ without affecting successful deliveries to Lambda and SQS subscribers. Enable SNS message filtering on the HTTP endpoint subsc is incorrect because message filtering is designed to route messages based on attributes, not to predict or prevent downstream errors from occurring.

question: A company uses SNS to distribute product price updates to 50 microservices. Each microservice only needs updates for its specific product category. Currently, all services receive all updates and filter locally, wasting resources. Which SNS feature reduces unnecessary message delivery?
options: A. Create separate SNS topics for each product category and have the publishing service send updates to the appropriate topic, with each microservice subscribing only to the topic for its product category

B. Implement a fan-out pattern with an SQS queue per microservice and add message filtering logic in each microservice consumer to discard irrelevant messages before processing the relevant category updates

C. Configure SNS message filtering policies on each subscription that match only the product category attribute relevant to that microservice, so SNS delivers only matching messages to each subscriber

D. Use SNS message attributes to tag each update with its product category and configure each microservice to check the attribute upon receipt, immediately discarding messages for categories it does not handle
explanation: Configure SNS message filtering policies on each subscr is correct because SNS subscription filter policies evaluate message attributes at the SNS service level and deliver only messages that match the filter criteria to each subscriber, eliminating unnecessary message delivery and processing. Create separate SNS topics for each product category is incorrect because creating 50 separate topics for each product category adds significant management overhead and requires the publisher to know about all topics.

question: A company uses SNS to send order confirmation messages to customers via SMS. The company expanded to international markets and needs to ensure SMS messages are delivered reliably to phone numbers in countries with strict carrier regulations. Which SNS SMS configuration improves delivery reliability?
options: A. Send all SMS messages through Amazon SES instead of SNS because SES provides better international SMS delivery rates and supports more carrier networks globally compared to the SNS SMS delivery system

B. Configure the SNS topic to use a FIFO delivery mode for SMS messages, which guarantees ordered delivery and ensures no messages are lost during delivery to international phone numbers with strict regulations

C. Configure the SNS SMS message type as Transactional instead of Promotional, which uses higher-quality delivery routes with higher per-message costs but provides better delivery rates for critical messages

D. Increase the SNS SMS spending limit to the maximum allowed value so that message delivery is never throttled due to budget constraints, ensuring all order confirmations reach customers regardless of total volume
explanation: Configure the SNS SMS message type as Transactional ins is correct because setting the SMS message type to Transactional prioritizes delivery reliability over cost by using premium delivery routes that have higher success rates, which is important for critical messages like order confirmations in regulated markets. Send all SMS messages through Amazon SES instead of SNS is incorrect because SES is an email service and does not send SMS messages.

question: A company publishes high-priority alerts and low-priority notifications to the same SNS topic. During traffic spikes, the high volume of low-priority notifications delays delivery of high-priority alerts. The company needs to ensure high-priority alerts are delivered with minimal latency. Which solution addresses this problem?
options: A. Increase the SNS topic throughput limit by contacting AWS Support to request a higher message publish rate, ensuring both high and low priority messages can be processed quickly without any delivery delays

B. Separate high-priority alerts and low-priority notifications into two different SNS topics so that high-priority alert delivery is not affected by the volume of low-priority notifications during traffic spikes

C. Configure SNS message filtering with a priority attribute and set up a filter policy that gives delivery preference to messages with a high-priority attribute, delaying low-priority messages during peak periods

D. Enable SNS message batching to combine multiple low-priority notifications into single batch deliveries, reducing the total number of publish operations and freeing capacity for high-priority alert messages
explanation: Separate high-priority alerts is correct because separating messages into different topics ensures that the high volume of low-priority notifications on one topic does not contend with or delay the delivery of high-priority alerts on the dedicated topic. Increase the SNS topic throughput limit by contacting A is incorrect because increasing throughput limits does not provide delivery prioritization and both message types still compete for the same delivery pipeline.

question: A company operates a latency-sensitive trading application deployed in us-east-1 and eu-west-1. Users across the globe connect directly to Regional ALB endpoints, and those far from both Regions experience high latency. The company needs to reduce network latency for all users without changing the application architecture. Which service addresses this?
options: A. Configure AWS Global Accelerator with endpoints in both Regions so that user traffic enters the AWS global network at the nearest edge location and is routed over the optimized AWS backbone to the closest healthy Region

B. Deploy Amazon CloudFront distributions in front of the ALBs in both Regions and configure the distributions to cache API responses at edge locations, reducing round-trip latency for repeated requests globally

C. Create Route 53 latency-based routing records pointing to the ALBs in both Regions so that DNS resolution directs users to the Region with the lowest network latency based on the resolver geographic location

D. Deploy additional ALBs in four more Regions and use Route 53 geolocation routing to direct users to the nearest Regional ALB, reducing the distance between users and the application entry point globally
explanation: Configure AWS Global Accelerator is correct because global Accelerator uses AWS edge locations to onramp user traffic to the AWS global network at the nearest point of presence, then routes it over the optimized AWS backbone to the endpoint in the closest healthy Region, reducing internet-based latency. Deploy Amazon CloudFront distributions in front of the is incorrect because cloudFront is designed for cacheable content and a trading application with dynamic, personalized API responses would not benefit from caching.

question: A gaming company uses Global Accelerator to route players to the nearest Regional game server. The company is launching a new Region in ap-southeast-1 and wants to gradually shift traffic to the new Region to validate performance before routing all Asia Pacific players there. Which Global Accelerator feature supports this gradual migration?
options: A. Configure endpoint group traffic dials to control the percentage of traffic routed to the new ap-southeast-1 endpoint group, starting at a low percentage and increasing it as confidence in the new Region grows

B. Create a separate Global Accelerator accelerator for the new Region and use Route 53 weighted routing between the two accelerator DNS names to gradually shift traffic from the existing accelerator to the new one

C. Add the new Region endpoints to the existing endpoint group and configure endpoint weights to send a small percentage of traffic to the new endpoints while the majority continues to the established Region endpoints

D. Use Global Accelerator custom routing to define specific port mappings that direct a subset of player connections to the new Region based on their source port range while routing other players to existing Regions
explanation: Configure endpoint group traffic dials to control the p is correct because the traffic dial on an endpoint group allows controlling the percentage of traffic that Global Accelerator routes to that group, enabling gradual migration by starting with a small percentage and incrementally increasing it. Create a separate Global Accelerator accelerator for th is incorrect because creating a separate accelerator and using Route 53 routing adds complexity and loses the benefits of Global Accelerator health checking and traffic management within a single accelerator.

question: A financial services company requires static IP addresses for its global API endpoints so that partner organizations can add them to firewall allow lists. The API is deployed behind ALBs in three Regions. Partners currently maintain separate allow lists for each Regional ALB IP, which changes periodically. Which solution provides stable static IPs for all Regions?
options: A. Deploy AWS Global Accelerator which provides two static anycast IP addresses that serve as the global entry point, routing traffic to the nearest healthy Regional ALB without partners needing Regional IPs
B. Assign Elastic IP addresses to each Regional ALB and provide these EIPs to partners for their firewall allow lists, since EIPs remain static unless explicitly released by the account owner

C. Configure Route 53 with an alias record pointing to each ALB and provide the Route 53 DNS name to partners, since DNS names remain constant even when the underlying ALB IP addresses change periodically

D. Deploy Network Load Balancers with Elastic IPs in each Region and place them in front of the ALBs, providing partners with the three sets of NLB static IPs for their firewall allow lists across all Regions
explanation: Deploy AWS Global Accelerator is correct because global Accelerator provides two static anycast IP addresses that never change and serve as a single global entry point, simplifying partner firewall configurations to just two IPs regardless of how many Regions the application spans. Assign Elastic IP addresses to each Regional ALB is incorrect because partners need IP addresses for firewall allow lists and DNS names cannot be used in IP-based firewall rules. Configure Route 53 is incorrect because aLBs do not support Elastic IP assignment, as ALB IP addresses are managed dynamically by AWS.

question: A company uses Global Accelerator for its multi-Region application. The us-east-1 endpoint group experiences a partial failure where the ALB is healthy but serving errors for some request paths. Global Accelerator health checks only verify basic connectivity. The company needs more granular failover control. Which approach provides application-aware failover?
options: A. Configure Global Accelerator health checks to use a custom health check endpoint that validates critical application paths and returns unhealthy when any monitored path fails, triggering failover to another Region

B. Deploy a Lambda function that periodically tests critical application paths and uses the Global Accelerator API to remove the unhealthy endpoint group when failures are detected on specific request paths

C. Use Route 53 health checks in front of Global Accelerator that monitor specific application paths, and configure Route 53 to update Global Accelerator endpoint weights automatically based on health check results

D. Configure the ALB health check to verify all critical application paths and rely on Global Accelerator to detect the ALB target group health status, which triggers automatic failover to the secondary Region endpoint
explanation: Deploy a Lambda function is correct because a Lambda function can perform deep application health checks on specific paths and use the Global Accelerator API to adjust traffic away from the partially failing endpoint group, providing application-aware failover control. Configure Global Accelerator health checks to use a cus is incorrect because route 53 health checks cannot directly modify Global Accelerator endpoint configurations, and the two services do not integrate in this manner.

question: A multiplayer game company uses Global Accelerator to route players to game server EC2 instances. Each game session runs on a specific instance and players must connect to that exact instance, not be load balanced. The company needs Global Accelerator to provide static IPs while allowing deterministic routing to specific instances. Which accelerator type supports this?
options: A. Use a standard Global Accelerator with endpoint weights set to route 100 percent of traffic to a single instance within each endpoint group, reconfiguring the weights when new game sessions are created

B. Use Global Accelerator custom routing which maps specific port ranges to specific EC2 instances, allowing the game matchmaking service to assign players a port that deterministically routes them to their game server

C. Use a standard Global Accelerator with multiple listener ports, one per game server instance, and configure each listener to forward traffic to a single-instance endpoint group for deterministic routing

D. Use a standard Global Accelerator with client affinity set to SOURCE_IP so that once a player connects to a specific game server instance, all subsequent traffic from that player routes to the same instance
explanation: Use Global Accelerator custom routing is correct because global Accelerator custom routing accelerators provide deterministic mapping from specific port ranges on the static accelerator IPs to specific EC2 instance destinations, allowing the matchmaking service to assign a port that routes the player to their assigned game server. Use a standard Global Accelerator is incorrect because standard accelerators have limits on the number of listeners and this approach does not scale to hundreds or thousands of game server instances.

question: A company runs critical on-premises applications and needs a disaster recovery solution that provides an RPO of seconds and an RTO of minutes. The company wants to minimize the cost of maintaining DR infrastructure in AWS when no disaster is occurring. Which service meets these requirements?
options: A. Deploy identical production infrastructure in AWS using CloudFormation and keep it running at all times in a warm standby configuration, using Route 53 failover routing to switch traffic during a disaster event
B. Create nightly AMI snapshots of all on-premises servers using the VM Import/Export service and store them in AWS, launching instances from the most recent AMIs during a disaster for recovery

C. Use AWS Backup to create hourly backups of on-premises servers and store them in S3, restoring from the latest backup to new EC2 instances when a disaster occurs to recover the application environment

D. Use AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery to continuously replicate on-premises servers to AWS with lightweight staging instances, launching full-sized recovery instances only when a disaster is declared to minimize idle costs
explanation: Use AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery to continuously repli is correct because AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery continuously replicates data at the block level providing seconds of RPO, uses low-cost staging instances during normal operations to minimize costs, and can launch full-sized recovery instances within minutes providing a low RTO. Deploy identical production infrastructure in AWS is incorrect because keeping full production infrastructure running at all times in warm standby is significantly more expensive than Elastic Disaster Recovery staging instances.

question: A company uses AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery to protect its on-premises VMware environment. The team wants to verify that their DR plan works correctly before an actual disaster. Testing must not affect the production environment or the ongoing replication. Which Elastic Disaster Recovery capability supports this requirement?
options: A. Pause replication temporarily and launch recovery instances from the current replication state, conduct testing on the launched instances, then resume replication after the test is complete and results are validated

B. Initiate a DR drill that launches recovery instances from the replicated data in an isolated VPC, allowing the team to test the recovered environment without interrupting ongoing replication or affecting production

C. Create manual EBS snapshots of the replication volumes and launch EC2 instances from these snapshots in a separate account, testing the recovery procedure while replication continues to the original volumes

D. Redirect a small percentage of production traffic to the AWS DR environment using Route 53 weighted routing to validate that the replicated servers can handle real production traffic without fully failing over
explanation: Initiate a DR drill is correct because elastic Disaster Recovery supports non-disruptive DR drills that launch test instances from replicated data without affecting ongoing replication or the production source environment, allowing teams to validate their recovery plan safely. Pause replication temporarily is incorrect because pausing replication creates a gap in data protection during the test period, which is risky for production environments. Create manual EBS snapshots of the replication volumes is incorrect because manually creating snapshots and launching instances in another account adds significant operational complexity compared to the native drill feature.

question: A company has completed a disaster recovery failover to AWS using Elastic Disaster Recovery. Production is now running on AWS recovery instances. The on-premises data center has been repaired and the company wants to fail back to the original on-premises environment. Which approach returns operations to on-premises?
options: A. Use Elastic Disaster Recovery failback feature to reverse replication from the AWS recovery instances back to the on-premises servers, then perform a cutover back to on-premises once replication is synchronized

B. Create AMIs of the AWS recovery instances and use VM Import/Export to convert them to VMware virtual machine images, then deploy the converted images on the repaired on-premises VMware infrastructure

C. Set up a new Elastic Disaster Recovery configuration that treats the on-premises environment as the target and AWS as the source, initiating fresh replication from AWS to on-premises before cutting over
D. Manually copy all data from the AWS recovery instances to the on-premises servers using AWS DataSync over a Direct Connect link, then shut down the AWS instances and resume operations on-premises
explanation: Use Elastic Disaster Recovery failback feature to rever is correct because elastic Disaster Recovery includes a native failback capability that reverses the replication direction from AWS back to the on-premises environment, synchronizing data changes made during the failover period before performing a clean cutover. Create AMIs of the AWS recovery instances is incorrect because VM Import/Export conversion is a manual process that does not capture real-time changes and may result in compatibility issues with the on-premises VMware environment.

question: A company protects 50 on-premises servers with AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery. The security team requires that all replicated data stored in AWS be encrypted using customer-managed KMS keys and that replication traffic be encrypted in transit. Which configuration meets both encryption requirements?
options: A. Configure the Elastic Disaster Recovery replication settings to use customer-managed KMS keys for EBS volume encryption on staging and recovery instances, and the service encrypts replication traffic in transit by default

B. Create encrypted EBS volumes manually using customer-managed KMS keys and configure Elastic Disaster Recovery to use these pre-created volumes as replication targets for each of the 50 on-premises servers

C. Configure the replication agent on each on-premises server to encrypt data using the customer-managed KMS key before transmitting it to AWS, and store the encrypted data on unencrypted EBS volumes in AWS

D. Use AWS CloudHSM to generate and manage encryption keys for both in-transit and at-rest encryption, configuring the Elastic Disaster Recovery replication agent to use the CloudHSM endpoint for key operations
explanation: Configure the Elastic Disaster Recovery replication set is correct because elastic Disaster Recovery supports specifying customer-managed KMS keys for EBS volume encryption in the replication settings, and the service encrypts all replication data in transit using TLS by default without additional configuration. Create encrypted EBS volumes manually is incorrect because elastic Disaster Recovery manages replication volumes automatically and does not support using pre-created EBS volumes as replication targets.

question: A company uses Elastic Disaster Recovery and needs to ensure that when a failover is initiated, the recovery instances launch with the correct network configuration including specific VPC, subnets, security groups, and instance types. Where are these recovery environment settings configured?
options: A. Create a CloudFormation template that defines the VPC, subnets, security groups, and instance types, and trigger the template deployment as a post-recovery step after Elastic Disaster Recovery launches instances

B. Configure the network settings in the Elastic Disaster Recovery replication agent installed on each on-premises server, which transmits the desired AWS network configuration along with the replication data

C. Configure launch settings in the Elastic Disaster Recovery console for each source server, specifying the target VPC, subnet, security groups, and instance type that recovery instances should use when launched

D. Create an AWS Systems Manager runbook that modifies the network configuration of recovery instances after they launch, moving them to the correct VPC and attaching the specified security groups and subnets
explanation: Configure launch settings in the Elastic Disaster Recov is correct because elastic Disaster Recovery launch settings allow configuring the target EC2 instance type, VPC, subnet, security groups, and other parameters for each source server, ensuring recovery instances launch with the correct network configuration automatically. Create a CloudFormation template that defines the VPC is incorrect because using CloudFormation as a post-recovery step delays proper network configuration and may leave instances temporarily misconfigured during the critical recovery period.

question: A media company stores millions of image assets in Amazon S3. The application performs thousands of GET requests per second spread across random object key prefixes. Users report intermittent HTTP 503 Slow Down errors during peak hours. Which solution best improves read performance while minimizing application changes?
options: A. Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket and update the application endpoint to use the accelerated domain for all GET requests
B. Add randomized four-character hex prefixes to object keys and re-upload all objects so that requests are spread across more S3 partitions
C. Migrate the objects to S3 Intelligent-Tiering and enable the Archive Access tier to redistribute the storage load across partitions
D. Place Amazon CloudFront in front of the S3 bucket with a high TTL cache policy so repeated object reads are served from edge locations
explanation: Place Amazon CloudFront in front is correct because cloudFront caches frequently accessed objects at edge locations, offloading the majority of GET requests from S3 and eliminating throttling errors. Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on is incorrect because enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket with Transfer Acceleration speeds up uploads. Add randomized four-character hex prefixes is incorrect because add randomized four-character hex prefixes to object keys with randomized prefixes was a legacy best practice. Migrate the objects to S3 is incorrect because migrate the objects to S3 Intelligent-Tiering with Intelligent-Tiering manages storage cost tiers.

question: A genomics research firm uploads multi-terabyte sequencing datasets from on-premises servers to Amazon S3 over a 1 Gbps Direct Connect link. Single-object uploads frequently time out before completing. Which approach maximizes upload throughput and reliability for these large files?
options: A. Enable S3 Versioning on the destination bucket and retry each failed upload so that partial data is preserved and can be resumed later
B. Use S3 multipart upload with a part size of 100 MB and configure parallel thread uploads to maximize bandwidth utilization across the link
C. Compress each dataset into a single gzip archive before uploading to reduce the amount of data transferred across the Direct Connect link
D. Switch from Direct Connect to an AWS Site-to-Site VPN connection which provides built-in packet retransmission for large object transfers
explanation: Use S3 multipart upload with is correct because S3 multipart upload breaks large objects into smaller parts that upload in parallel, maximizing throughput and allowing failed parts to retry independently. Enable S3 Versioning on the is incorrect because enable S3 Versioning on the destination bucket with versioning does not resume partial uploads since a. Compress each dataset into a is incorrect because compress each dataset into a single gzip archive before with compression reduces size. Switch from Direct Connect to is incorrect because switch from Direct Connect to an AWS Site-to-Site VPN c with VPN provides lower throughput than.

question: An e-commerce platform serves product images from Amazon S3 to customers worldwide. Latency for customers in Asia-Pacific is three times higher than for North American users. The company wants to reduce image load times globally without replicating the bucket to other Regions. Which solution provides the lowest latency improvement?
options: A. Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket and update the application to generate pre-signed URLs that reference the accelerated endpoint
B. Create an Amazon CloudFront distribution with the S3 bucket as origin and configure a cache policy with a 24-hour default TTL for images
C. Enable S3 Cross-Region Replication to an Asia-Pacific Region bucket and use Route 53 latency-based routing to direct users accordingly
